title: Complete Writer Service Implementation
description:
## Problem

The Writer service is currently a placeholder implementation. The write path is incomplete, preventing data ingestion functionality.

## Scope

Implement full write path with data ingestion capabilities.

### Core Functionality

* Implement INSERT operations
* Support batch loading
* Support streaming writes
* Coordinate with Metastore to register new data parts
* Write data parts to S3 storage
* Handle write errors gracefully

### Integration Points

* gRPC service implementation
* Metastore coordination for metadata updates
* S3 object storage writes
* DataFusion integration for data transformation

### Testing

* Unit tests for write operations
* Integration tests for INSERT statements
* Error handling tests
* Performance tests for bulk loading

## Affected Files

* `writer/` - Main implementation
* `proto/` - gRPC service definitions
* `metastore/` - Coordination logic

## Success Criteria

* INSERT statements work end-to-end
* Data can be loaded and queried back
* Write performance is acceptable (baseline TBD)
* Proper error handling for write failures

## Effort

\~1-2 weeks

title: Add Authentication Layer (API Keys/JWT)
description:
## Problem

No authentication or authorization exists. All endpoints are publicly accessible without security.

## Scope

Implement basic authentication to secure access to KaonDB.

### Phase 1: API Key Authentication

* Create auth module/crate
* Implement API key generation and management
* Add middleware for HTTP endpoint authentication
* Add gRPC interceptor for service authentication
* Store API keys securely (hashed)
* Configuration for auth settings

### Phase 2: JWT Support (Optional for MVP)

* JWT token generation
* Token validation and refresh
* Claims-based authorization

### Integration Points

* Frontend HTTP endpoints (`POST /query`)
* Metastore gRPC service
* Worker gRPC service
* Writer gRPC service
* CLI authentication

## Security Considerations

* Hash API keys (bcrypt/argon2)
* Support API key rotation
* Rate limiting per key
* Audit logging of authentication events

## Affected Files

* New: `auth/` crate
* `frontend/` - HTTP authentication
* `worker/`, `metastore/`, `writer/` - gRPC authentication
* `cli/` - Client authentication
* `config/` - Auth configuration

## Success Criteria

* All endpoints require valid authentication
* Invalid credentials are rejected with clear error messages
* Performance impact <5ms per request

## Effort

\~1-2 weeks

title: Implement Backup and Restore Functionality
description:
## Problem

No backup or restore capabilities exist. Data durability procedures are missing.

## Scope

Implement snapshot-based backup and restore for KaonDB metadata and data.

### Backup Features

* Full snapshot backup to S3
* Incremental backups (optimization)
* Backup scheduling/automation
* Backup verification
* Backup retention policies
* Backup metadata management

### Restore Features

* Restore from full backup
* Point-in-time recovery (PITR)
* Restore to different cluster
* Restore validation
* Dry-run mode

### CLI Commands

* `kaondb backup create`
* `kaondb backup list`
* `kaondb backup restore`
* `kaondb backup verify`
* `kaondb backup delete`

### Testing

* Backup and restore integration tests
* Corruption detection tests
* Restore to different environment
* Performance benchmarks

## Affected Files

* `metastore/` - Snapshot backup logic
* `cli/` - Backup/restore commands
* New: `backup/` crate (optional)

## Success Criteria

* Full backup and restore working
* Automated testing
* Recovery time objective (RTO) < 1 hour for SF1

## Effort

\~1-2 weeks

title: Stripe billing: subscription checkout, webhook handler, access gating
description:
## Goal

Integrate Stripe for subscription billing. MVP scope: one plan, monthly billing, per-org pricing.

## Design decisions

* **Stripe Checkout** for the payment flow — no custom payment forms
* **ClickHouse for billing state** — append-only `ReplacingMergeTree` transitions table for the audit log + a `HASHED` Dictionary (`LIFETIME 0–30s`) for O(1) current-state lookups from the auth middleware
* `async-stripe` **crate** for Rust ↔ Stripe API interactions
* Webhook handler is the source of truth for subscription activation (not the checkout redirect)

## Sub-tasks

* [VRN-53](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-53/stripe-account-setup-and-product-configuration) — Stripe account setup & product configuration (manual)
* [VRN-54](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-54/clickhouse-billing-state-schema-transitions-table-dictionary) — ClickHouse billing state schema: transitions table + dictionary
* [VRN-55](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-55/stripe-checkout-session-flow) — Stripe Checkout session flow
* [VRN-56](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-56/stripe-webhook-handler) — Stripe webhook handler
* [VRN-57](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-57/subscription-access-gating-middleware) — Subscription access gating middleware

## Dependency graph

```
VRN-53 (account setup)
  ├──▶ VRN-55 (checkout flow)
  └──▶ VRN-56 (webhook handler)

VRN-54 (CH schema)
  ├──▶ VRN-56 (webhook handler)
  └──▶ VRN-57 (access gating)
```

[VRN-53](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-53/stripe-account-setup-and-product-configuration) and [VRN-54](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-54/clickhouse-billing-state-schema-transitions-table-dictionary) can proceed in parallel. [VRN-55](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-55/stripe-checkout-session-flow)/56/57 follow once their dependencies land.

## Acceptance criteria

* User can subscribe via Stripe Checkout
* Subscription status reflected in the app (active/inactive)
* Cancelled subscriptions handled gracefully (grace period, not hard cutoff)
* Stripe webhook handler processes lifecycle events reliably
* Access gating enforced across API and UI

title: Deploy Rafn to staging and production environments
description:
Deploy the Rafn stack (API, frontend, ClickHouse, Zitadel) to a real environment that external users can access.

Staging first, then prod. Use the existing Docker setup as a starting point. Target Hetzner or [Fly.io](<http://Fly.io>) for the API/frontend, Elestio or ClickHouse Cloud for the database, Zitadel Cloud for auth (already scoped in [VRN-16](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-16/provision-zitadel-cloud-eu-instance-for-staging-document-self-host)).

Needs: container orchestration (docker-compose on a VPS or k8s-lite), TLS termination, DNS for api.rafn.dev + app.rafn.dev, secrets management, automated deploys from main branch.

Acceptance criteria:

* Staging environment accessible at staging.rafn.dev (or similar)
* Automated deploy on merge to main
* TLS everywhere
* Health checks and basic uptime monitoring
* Production environment follows once staging is validated

title: GitHub App: register app, post PR comments with benchmark deltas
description:
Register a GitHub App for Rafn that receives webhook events and posts PR comments with benchmark comparison results.

Core flow:

1. User installs the Rafn GitHub App on their repo
2. When benchmark data is uploaded for a PR commit (via [VRN-42](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-42/github-action-rafn-devaction-wrapping-cli-upload-for-ci)), Rafn compares against the base branch
3. Rafn posts/updates a PR comment showing a table of benchmark deltas — regressions in red, improvements in green, stable in gray
4. Comment updates on subsequent pushes to the same PR

The GitHub App needs `pull_requests: write` and `checks: write` permissions. Authentication via GitHub App installation tokens.

Consider whether to trigger comparison on webhook (`push` / `pull_request`) or reactively when benchmark data arrives via the ingestion endpoint. Reactive (data-driven) is simpler for MVP — no webhook infrastructure needed, just post the comment after ingestion completes if there's a matching open PR.

Acceptance criteria:

* GitHub App registered and installable
* PR comment posted with regression/improvement table after benchmark upload
* Comment updates on re-push (upsert, not duplicate)
* Comment links back to the Rafn dashboard for the run

title: Regression detection: threshold-based comparison between commits
description:
Compare benchmark results between two commits (typically PR head vs base branch) and flag regressions/improvements.

MVP approach: percentage threshold on mean values. Given a base commit's benchmarks and a head commit's benchmarks, compute the relative change per benchmark. Flag as regression if change exceeds a configurable threshold (default 5%).

Query ClickHouse for the base branch's latest results for the same benchmark set, compare against the incoming run.

This powers both the PR comment (VRN-44) and the dashboard regression indicators.

Acceptance criteria:

* Given two sets of benchmark results, produces a comparison report with per-benchmark deltas
* Configurable threshold (per-project setting)
* Handles missing benchmarks (new benchmark, removed benchmark) gracefully
* Unit tests with synthetic data covering regression, improvement, and noise scenarios

title: CLI `rafn upload` command: parse, collect context, ship to API
description:
`rafn upload` CLI command that:

1. Auto-detects benchmark output format (Criterion first, extensible later)
2. Parses results using the parser from [VRN-39](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-39/criterion-output-parser-read-targetcriterion-json-into-proto-format)
3. Collects git context (commit SHA, branch, parent SHAs) from the local repo
4. Collects machine info (CPU count, model, OS, arch)
5. Authenticates (OIDC token or API key from env)
6. Uploads the `BenchmarkSet` to the ingestion endpoint ([VRN-40](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-40/ingestion-api-endpoint-receive-benchmark-data-write-to-clickhouse))

This is what users run at the end of their CI pipeline: `rafn upload --criterion-dir target/criterion`.

Acceptance criteria:

* Uploads Criterion results to a running Rafn API instance
* Populates git and machine context automatically
* Clear error messages on auth failure, missing data, network errors
* `--dry-run` flag to preview what would be sent

title: Ingestion API endpoint: receive benchmark data, write to ClickHouse
description:
Server-side endpoint in `rafn-api` that receives a `BenchmarkSet` proto payload (or JSON equivalent), validates it, resolves the project/tenant context, and writes the benchmark samples to ClickHouse.

The endpoint is authenticated via OIDC token or API key. Tenant and project are resolved from the token claims + request metadata.

This is the write path that the CLI uploader and GitHub Action will hit.

Acceptance criteria:

* POST endpoint accepts benchmark data and persists to `benchmark_samples` table
* Tenant isolation enforced — can't write to another tenant's project
* Returns structured error on validation failure
* Integration test against local ClickHouse

project_milestone.name: M2 — Ingestion & storage (Rust)
project_milestone.description: End-to-end path from a developer's `cargo bench` run to persisted time-series data: cargo bench result parser, common internal benchmark format, ClickHouse schema, ingestion API, and client-side shipper (CLI + GitHub Action) for Rust projects.

title: Criterion output parser: read target/criterion JSON into proto format
description:
Parse Criterion's `target/criterion` output directory into the `BenchmarkSet` proto format defined in `benchmark.proto`.

Read the JSON reports Criterion writes per benchmark group (estimates.json, sample.json). Extract benchmark name, mean/median/stddev, individual samples, units, and iteration count. Map to proto `Benchmark` messages with `MetricStatistics` populated.

This is the first language adapter — design the parser trait/interface so Go/Java/C# adapters follow the same pattern later.

Crate: `crates/rafn-parsers` or similar.

Acceptance criteria:

* Given a real Criterion output directory, produces a valid `BenchmarkSet` proto
* Handles missing optional fields gracefully
* Unit tests against fixture data from a real `cargo bench` run

title: Set up Zitadel identity provider for Rafn
description:
## Goal

Umbrella ticket for all Zitadel identity-provider setup work in Rafn's M1 — Foundation milestone.

## Structure

Provider-specific work lives in sub-issues:

* **ADR-0001**: document the identity-provider decision — [VRN-17](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-17/adr-0001-document-identity-provider-decision-zitadel)
* **Provision Zitadel Cloud (EU)** for staging, document self-host migration path — [VRN-16](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-16/provision-zitadel-cloud-eu-instance-for-staging-document-self-host)
* **Configure Zitadel as code**: projects, organizations, applications, tenant mapping, dev users — [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications)

## Sequencing

The natural order is [VRN-17](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-17/adr-0001-document-identity-provider-decision-zitadel) (decide) → [VRN-16](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-16/provision-zitadel-cloud-eu-instance-for-staging-document-self-host) (provision cloud) → [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications) (configure as code). Local dev gets Zitadel in a container via [VRN-9](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-9/local-dev-environment-docker-compose-with-clickhouse-zitadel-just) in parallel.

## Acceptance criteria

* All sub-issues completed.
* Local dev, CI, staging all start from the same known Zitadel state.
* The API skeleton ([VRN-14](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-14/api-framework-skeleton-axum-service-with-oidc-auth-tenant-extraction)) can verify OIDC tokens and extract tenants against the configured instance.

title: API framework skeleton: axum service with OIDC auth, tenant extraction, healthz/readyz/me
description:
## Goal

Stand up the HTTP API service skeleton with OIDC token verification and tenant extraction on every request. The service does nothing useful yet (no domain endpoints), but it's the plumbing every M2+ ticket will plug into.

## Scope

### Crate: `crates/rafn-api`

Binary target. Depends on `rafn-core`, `rafn-auth`, `rafn-storage`, `rafn-config`.

### Framework

* `axum` 0.7.x for routing
* `tokio` runtime (multi-threaded)
* `tower-http` middleware stack:
  * `TraceLayer` for request logging
  * `CorsLayer` permissive in dev, configurable in prod
  * `CompressionLayer`
  * `RequestIdLayer` (custom or from tower-http)
  * `TimeoutLayer` — 30s default per request

### Endpoints (M1 scope)

* `GET /healthz` — liveness, returns `200` always
* `GET /readyz` — readiness, checks ClickHouse connectivity, returns `200`/`503`
* `GET /v1/me` — authenticated; returns the decoded principal (tenant_id, user_id, email, roles) from the access token
* `GET /v1/version` — returns git SHA + build timestamp (injected at build time via `build.rs` or env)

No domain endpoints (projects, runs, benchmarks). Those come in M2/M3.

### Auth middleware (`rafn-auth`)

* Extract bearer token from `Authorization` header
* Verify signature against Zitadel JWKS (`<issuer>/oauth/v2/keys`)
* Cache JWKS for 10 min with background refresh
* Validate `iss`, `aud`, `exp`:
  * `iss` — the configured Zitadel issuer URL (e.g. `http://zitadel:8080` in dev)
  * `aud` — must include the configured Rafn API audience (typically the `rafn-platform` project ID; see [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications))
  * `exp` — standard expiry check with small clock skew allowance (30s)
* Extract into a `Principal` struct: `{ user_id, tenant_id, email, roles: Vec<String> }`
  * `user_id` — Zitadel `sub` claim
  * `tenant_id` — custom claim injected by the Zitadel Action (org slug/domain, not Zitadel's internal org ID; see [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications))
  * `roles` — from Zitadel's `urn:zitadel:iam:org:project:roles` claim (the standard Zitadel project-roles claim format)
* Reject with 401 on missing/invalid token; 403 on valid token with missing `tenant_id` claim
* Make `Principal` available via axum extractor so handlers can declare `principal: Principal` in their signature

Implementation note: the OIDC plumbing should use `openidconnect` or `jsonwebtoken` + manual JWKS fetch. Zitadel is standards-compliant OIDC so no provider-specific SDK is needed.

### API token path (for CI)

* If the `Authorization` header is `Bearer rafn_<base64>`, treat as API token instead of JWT:
  * Hash the secret (SHA-256)
  * Look up in `api_tokens` table via `MetadataClient`
  * Construct a synthetic `Principal` from the token row
* Both auth paths converge on the same `Principal` type — handlers don't care which was used

### Error model

* A single `ApiError` type in `rafn-core` with `thiserror`
* Implements `IntoResponse` to produce consistent JSON error bodies: `{ "error": { "code": "string", "message": "string" } }`
* No panics reach the response layer — `unwrap`/`expect` are clippy-denied

### Tracing

* `tracing` with `tracing-subscriber` configured for JSON output in prod, pretty in dev
* Every request gets a span with `request_id`, `method`, `path`, and (after auth) `tenant_id`, `user_id`

### Tests

* Integration test (`crates/rafn-api/tests/auth.rs`):
  * Spin up test server with a mocked JWKS endpoint (no real Zitadel needed)
  * `GET /v1/me` without token → 401
  * `GET /v1/me` with forged token (wrong signature) → 401
  * `GET /v1/me` with valid token missing `tenant_id` → 403
  * `GET /v1/me` with valid token → 200, correct principal
  * Cover both a JWT (Zitadel-shaped) and a `rafn_<base64>` API token in separate tests
* `/healthz` and `/readyz` tested without auth

## Out of scope

* Rate limiting — M4
* Request body size limits beyond axum defaults — revisit per-endpoint in M2
* mTLS / client cert auth — not planned
* OpenAPI / docs generation — M3 when endpoints are worth documenting

## Acceptance criteria

* `cargo run -p rafn-api` boots against local compose stack and serves all 4 endpoints
* `curl http://localhost:3001/healthz` returns 200
* `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer $(just token alice)" http://localhost:3001/v1/me` returns the alice principal (where `just token alice` runs an authz-code flow against local Zitadel and prints the access token)
* All integration tests pass in CI using the mocked JWKS (no Zitadel container required in CI for these tests)
* Request logs are structured JSON in prod mode and include `request_id`
* Server shuts down gracefully on SIGTERM within 5s (in-flight requests finish, new ones rejected)

## Notes for agents

* Do not implement any domain endpoints in this ticket — the temptation will be strong, resist. M2 owns project/run/benchmark endpoints.
* `Principal` is a value type — don't leak axum types into `rafn-core`.
* JWKS cache invalidation on `kid` miss: if a token's `kid` isn't in the cache, force-refresh once before rejecting. This handles Zitadel key rotation gracefully.
* Zitadel's roles claim is `urn:zitadel:iam:org:project:roles` and is a JSON object (role name → org ID → org domain). Flatten to `Vec<String>` of role names for the `Principal`; keep the raw claim for debugging if useful.
* The issuer and audience must be configurable via env/config, not hardcoded — dev uses local Zitadel, staging/prod will use Zitadel Cloud URLs.

title: Zitadel instance configuration: projects, organizations, applications, tenant mapping, dev users
description:
## Goal

Zitadel is the identity provider (see the ADR ticket on provider selection). Configure it as code so dev, CI, and eventually prod all start from the same known state.

## Tenancy model (architectural decision)

Zitadel's hierarchy runs: Instance → Organization → Project → Application.

### Mapping for Rafn

* **Instance**: the Zitadel deployment itself — one per environment (dev-local, staging, prod).
* **Organization**: one Zitadel org per Rafn customer-tenant. The `dev` org represents Rafn tenant `dev`.
* **Project**: `rafn-platform` — the protected resource, owned by a dedicated system organization.
* **Applications** on `rafn-platform`:
  * `rafn-web` — OIDC public client, authz code + PKCE, for the Leptos UI (M3).
  * `rafn-cli` — OIDC public client, device code flow, for the CLI (M2).
  * `rafn-api` — API application for token audience validation.
* **Project roles**: `rafn-user` (default), `rafn-admin`.
* **Grants**: each customer organization is granted the `rafn-platform` project with roles assignable per-user.

The user's `tenant_id` claim is derived from their resource-owner organization, injected via a Zitadel Action (see "Custom claim" below). Emit the org's **slug/domain**, not the internal Zitadel org ID — this keeps Rafn's `tenant_id` domain concept decoupled from Zitadel's internal identifiers.

## Scope

### Configuration as code

Zitadel has no realm-JSON equivalent. Use a Rust xtask that calls the Zitadel Management API idempotently:

* Subcommand: `cargo run -p rafn-xtask -- zitadel-bootstrap`
* Declarative input: `infra/zitadel/config.toml`
* Applier: `crates/rafn-xtask/src/zitadel.rs`
* Check-before-create on every entity (org, project, app, user, role, action)
* REST API (not gRPC) to keep the xtask binary small — Zitadel exposes REST equivalents for all Management API ops

Terraform with the `zitadel/zitadel` provider is a valid alternative; deferred unless Terraform is already adopted elsewhere (M1 does not).

### Instance bootstrap (env-driven, at first boot)

Set on the Zitadel container startup:

* `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_ORG_HUMAN_USERNAME=admin@rafn.dev`
* `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_ORG_HUMAN_PASSWORD=<dev-only, documented>`
* `ZITADEL_EXTERNALDOMAIN=localhost`
* `ZITADEL_EXTERNALPORT=8080`
* `ZITADEL_EXTERNALSECURE=false` (dev only; prod is TLS)

A machine user and service-account key are issued at bootstrap so the xtask can authenticate to the Management API. The SA key JSON is written to `infra/zitadel/.admin-sa.json` (gitignored), consumed by the xtask, and regenerated on `just reset`.

### Entities applied by the xtask

1. **System org**: `rafn-system` (owns the platform project)
2. **Project**: `rafn-platform` on `rafn-system`
   * Roles: `rafn-user`, `rafn-admin`
   * Applications:
     * `rafn-web` — OIDC public, grant types: authz code + refresh; response type: code; PKCE required; redirect URIs: `http://localhost:3000/*` for dev
     * `rafn-cli` — OIDC public, grant types: device code + refresh; PKCE required
     * `rafn-api` — API app; token audience = project ID (or configured audience) for `aud` validation
3. **Customer org (dev)**: `dev`
   * Granted access to `rafn-platform` with role-assignment permissions
4. **Dev users in** `dev` org:
   * `admin@rafn.dev` / password `admin` — role `rafn-admin`
   * `alice@rafn.dev` / password `alice` — role `rafn-user`
   * Email pre-verified; no change-password-on-first-login in dev

### Custom claim (`tenant_id`)

Zitadel Actions (JavaScript hooks) inject custom claims. Create a post-authentication action on `rafn-platform`:

* Triggers: `post userinfo` and `post access token`
* Script emits `tenant_id` equal to the user's resource-owner organization's **slug/domain** (not the raw ID)
* Source: `infra/zitadel/actions/inject_tenant_id.js` (committed)
* Applied by the xtask (idempotent create-or-update)

### Backend integration points (consumed by [VRN-14](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-14/api-framework-skeleton-axum-service-with-oidc-auth-tenant-extraction))

* OIDC discovery: `http://zitadel:8080/.well-known/openid-configuration`
* Issuer: `http://zitadel:8080` (Zitadel issues at the root — no `/realms/x` path)
* JWKS: `http://zitadel:8080/oauth/v2/keys`
* Expected audience: `rafn-platform` project ID (or the configured API audience string)

### Docs

`docs/auth.md` covering:

* Architecture rationale (link ADR), org-per-tenant model, Zitadel vs. Rafn DB ownership split
* How to log in locally (which user, which app, which flow)
* How to re-run the bootstrap xtask after config changes
* Production considerations: Zitadel Cloud for Phase 0 vs. self-host migration (link cloud tenant ticket)
* The `tenant_id` claim: where it comes from, how to swap the source without breaking clients

## Out of scope

* Self-service signup flow — M3/M4
* Social login (Google/GitHub) — defer
* SCIM provisioning — defer
* SAML SSO for enterprise customers — \~12-month horizon, separate future ticket
* Production deployment (HA, TLS, custom domain) — covered by the Zitadel Cloud provisioning ticket and M4 self-host migration

## Acceptance criteria

* `just up` brings Zitadel up with the instance auto-initialized and the admin SA key written to `infra/zitadel/.admin-sa.json`
* `just zitadel-bootstrap` creates the system org, project, applications, dev org, dev users, roles, and `tenant_id` action — and is idempotent on re-run
* `curl -s http://localhost:8080/.well-known/openid-configuration | jq .issuer` returns `http://localhost:8080`
* An authz-code flow as `alice@rafn.dev` against `rafn-web` yields an access token whose decoded claims include `tenant_id = "dev"` and `aud` containing the `rafn-api` audience
* The xtask is runnable against an arbitrary Zitadel instance via `--base-url` and `--sa-key` flags — no `localhost` hardcoding

## Notes for agents

* Do not store production secrets in committed config. Dev-only credentials must be obviously dev-only.
* Pin the Zitadel image tag exactly in the compose file (set by the dev-env ticket); no `latest`.
* The `tenant_id` Action source lives in-repo; the xtask POSTs it to Zitadel. Do not author scripts only inside the Zitadel console.
* Use REST (not gRPC) from the xtask unless a gRPC client is already a dep.
* Emit the org's **slug/domain** as `tenant_id`, not Zitadel's internal org ID — internal IDs are Zitadel's, slugs are ours.
* Zitadel switched to AGPL-3.0 in 2025. Running unmodified Zitadel as auth infra is fine for Rafn's use; if we ever fork it, modifications must be disclosed.

title: CI pipeline: GitHub Actions (fmt, clippy, test, build) with rust-cache
description:
## Goal

Enforce code quality on every push and PR. Build must be green before merge; lint and format violations fail the build.

## Scope

### Workflow: `.github/workflows/ci.yml`

Triggers:

* `push` to `main`
* `pull_request` (all branches)

Jobs:

1. **fmt** — `cargo fmt --all -- --check`
2. **clippy** — `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`
3. **test** — `cargo test --workspace --all-features`
4. **build** — `cargo build --workspace --release` (smoke; artifacts not uploaded in M1)

Jobs run in parallel where independent. `test` depends on nothing; it should not wait on `clippy`.

### Runner & toolchain

* `ubuntu-24.04` runner
* Pin Rust via `rust-toolchain.toml` at repo root (stable channel, specific version e.g. `1.83.0`)
* Use `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable` with components `rustfmt, clippy`

### Caching

* Use `Swatinem/rust-cache@v2` with default settings
* Cache key should include the workspace manifest hash so a `Cargo.lock` change invalidates correctly

### Services

* CI jobs that touch ClickHouse: spin up `clickhouse/clickhouse-server:24.8` as a GitHub Actions service container (no Keycloak needed for unit/integration tests that don't hit auth)
* Tests that require auth mock the OIDC validation layer — we do **not** boot Keycloak in CI in M1

### Branch protection (document, don't configure in code)

* `main` requires: all 4 jobs green, 1 review, linear history
* Documented in `docs/ci.md`; actual branch protection config happens in GitHub settings

## Out of scope

* Release automation / publishing (M4)
* Security scanning (cargo-audit, cargo-deny) — separate follow-up ticket, not M1 blocker
* Coverage reporting — defer
* Docker image builds — defer to M2/M4

## Acceptance criteria

* PR with a `dbg!()` or `unwrap()` added to a non-test file fails `clippy` job
* PR with unformatted code fails `fmt` job
* PR with a broken test fails `test` job
* Warm-cache CI run completes in under 5 minutes
* Cold-cache CI run completes in under 12 minutes
* `docs/ci.md` explains how to reproduce each job locally via `just`

## Notes for agents

* Do not add `-D warnings` to `rustflags` globally; keep it scoped to the clippy invocation so local `cargo check` stays ergonomic
* Avoid matrix builds in M1 — single Linux/stable target only. Multi-platform is M2+ concern

title: Local dev environment: docker-compose with ClickHouse + Zitadel + just tasks
description:
## Goal

One-command local development: a new contributor runs a single `just` task and gets a working ClickHouse + Zitadel stack that the API crate can talk to, seeded with a dev tenant and a dev user.

> ## Scope
>
> ### Compose stack (`docker-compose.dev.yml`)
>
> * **ClickHouse** (single-node):
>   * Image: `clickhouse/clickhouse-server:24.8` (LTS; pin exact version)
>   * Expose: `8123` (HTTP), `9000` (native)
>   * Volume: named volume for `/var/lib/clickhouse`
>   * Bind-mount `./infra/clickhouse/users.d/` and `./infra/clickhouse/config.d/` for overrides
>   * Enable `access_control_improvements.users_without_row_policies_can_read_rows = 0` (future-proofing for row policies)
> * **zitadel-db** (Postgres backing store for Zitadel):
>   * Image: `postgres:16-alpine` (pin exact minor version)
>   * Env: `POSTGRES_DB=zitadel`, `POSTGRES_USER=zitadel`, `POSTGRES_PASSWORD=zitadel-dev` (dev only)
>   * Volume: named volume for `/var/lib/postgresql/data`
>   * Healthcheck: `pg_isready -U zitadel`
> * **Zitadel** (single-node):
>   * Image: `ghcr.io/zitadel/zitadel` — pin to the latest stable v2 release at implementation time (no `latest`)
>   * Depends on `zitadel-db` being healthy
>   * Command: `start-from-init --masterkey <32-char-dev-masterkey> --tlsMode disabled`
>   * Expose: `8080`
>   * Env (non-exhaustive — complete set per Zitadel docs):
>     * `ZITADEL_DATABASE_POSTGRES_*` pointing at `zitadel-db`
>     * `ZITADEL_EXTERNALDOMAIN=localhost`
>     * `ZITADEL_EXTERNALPORT=8080`
>     * `ZITADEL_EXTERNALSECURE=false`
>     * `ZITADEL_TLS_ENABLED=false`
>     * `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_ORG_HUMAN_USERNAME=admin@rafn.dev`
>     * `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_ORG_HUMAN_PASSWORD=admin` (dev only)
>     * `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_ORG_HUMAN_PASSWORDCHANGEREQUIRED=false`
>     * `ZITADEL_FIRSTINSTANCE_MACHINEKEYPATH=/machinekey/zitadel-admin-sa.json`
>   * Bind-mount: `./infra/zitadel/.admin-sa.json` ↔ `/machinekey/zitadel-admin-sa.json` so the admin service-account key surfaces on the host for the bootstrap xtask (see [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications))
>   * The masterkey is a dev-only constant in the compose file (documented as such). Prod gets a real secret from an env var or secret manager.

### Task runner

Adopt `just` (preferred) with `justfile` at repo root. Tasks:

* `just up` — start stack
* `just down` — stop stack, preserve volumes
* `just reset` — stop stack, wipe volumes (including `infra/zitadel/.admin-sa.json`), restart
* `just logs [service]` — tail logs
* `just seed` — run seed script (below)
* `just zitadel-bootstrap` — run the Zitadel bootstrap xtask (creates orgs/projects/apps/users; see [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications))
* `just ch` — shell into ClickHouse client
* `just zitadel` — open Zitadel console in browser (`http://localhost:8080/ui/console`)

Rationale for `just` over `make`: cleaner syntax, cross-platform, already common in Rust ecosystem.

### Seed script

`scripts/dev_seed.rs` (a small binary in the `rafn-xtask` crate, or a standalone script):

* Waits for ClickHouse to be healthy (retry `SELECT 1` on `8123`)
* Applies the M1 ClickHouse schema (delegated to the migration tool — see [VRN-12](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-12/clickhouse-schema-v0-metadata-tables-tenants-users-projects-api-tokens))
* Inserts one dev tenant (`tenant_id = 'dev'`) and one dev project in the ClickHouse metadata tables
* Waits for Zitadel to be ready (GET `.well-known/openid-configuration` returns 200)
* Triggers `zitadel-bootstrap` to ensure orgs/users/apps exist

### Docs

`docs/dev-setup.md` covering:

* Prereqs (Docker Desktop / OrbStack / Podman; Rust toolchain; `just`)
* First-run flow
* How to reset state (and when to — e.g., after breaking schema changes)
* How to reach each service (URLs, default creds)
* Troubleshooting (port conflicts, volume corruption, stale SA key)

## Out of scope

* Production deployment manifests (separate later ticket; Phase 0 prod uses Zitadel Cloud — see the cloud tenant ticket)
* Observability stack (Prometheus/Grafana) — add in M4
* A Postgres/relational metadata DB for Rafn itself — explicitly deferred; Rafn metadata lives in ClickHouse per M1 decision. (The `zitadel-db` Postgres is Zitadel's backing store, not a Rafn metadata store.)

## Acceptance criteria

* Fresh checkout → `just up && just seed` succeeds in under 3 minutes on a warm Docker cache (extra time vs. prior Keycloak-based estimate accounts for Zitadel DB init)
* `curl http://localhost:8123/ping` returns `Ok.`
* `curl http://localhost:8080/.well-known/openid-configuration` returns 200 with valid OIDC metadata and issuer `http://localhost:8080`
* `infra/zitadel/.admin-sa.json` exists on the host after `just up` and is a valid Zitadel machine-user key JSON
* `just reset` fully wipes both ClickHouse and Zitadel state (volumes + SA key) and rebuilds without manual intervention
* `docs/dev-setup.md` is followed end-to-end by someone who has never touched the repo before (self-review: read it as if you didn't write it)

## Notes for agents

* All service versions pinned to exact tags — no `latest`
* Config is checked in under `infra/` and imported by compose, not baked into images
* Dev credentials are documented in `docs/dev-setup.md`; no secrets in compose files beyond hardcoded dev values
* The Zitadel masterkey must be exactly 32 characters. Use a visibly-dev string (e.g. `RafnDevMasterKey________________`).
* Zitadel's `start-from-init` is idempotent — safe to restart the container
* The `.admin-sa.json` bind-mount target path must exist before `just up` (touch it, or let the xtask create the directory)
* ClickHouse's volume is unrelated to Zitadel's Postgres volume — keep them distinctly named (`rafn_ch_data`, `rafn_zitadel_db_data`)

title: Reserve `rafn` name on crates.io, npm, and PyPI
description:
## Goal

Reserve the `rafn` package name across the initial language/package ecosystems so the project has a clean namespace and avoids supply-chain confusion.

## Structure

Provider-specific work is tracked in sub-issues:

* [VRN-31](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-31/reserve-rafn-on-cratesio) — Reserve `rafn` on [crates.io](<http://crates.io>)
* [VRN-32](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-32/reserve-rafn-on-npm) — Reserve `rafn` on npm
* [VRN-33](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-33/reserve-rafn-on-pypi) — Reserve `rafn` on PyPI

This parent issue is complete when all three registry reservations are complete.

## Acceptance criteria

* `cargo search rafn`, `npm view rafn`, and `pip index versions rafn` all show placeholders owned by Voron Software.
* All registry entries link back to `https://rafn.dev` and the GitHub organization.
* 2FA is enabled on all relevant registry accounts.
* Ownership is tied to Voron Software rather than a personal-only account.

## Dependency

Blocked by [VRN-5](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-5/acquire-rafndev-domain) only for the canonical homepage URL in package metadata. If name squatting risk becomes urgent, individual sub-issues may use a temporary placeholder URL and update metadata later.

title: Acquire rafn.dev domain
description:
## Goal

Own `rafn.dev` and configure baseline DNS so we can publish the landing page and set up branded email.

## Scope

* Purchase `rafn.dev` via Cloudflare Registrar (preferred; Google charges fixed registry fee passthrough, CF is at-cost).
* Register in the name of **Voron Software GmbH** (company-owned, not personal).
* Enable registrar lock and auto-renew (at least 2 years).
* Enable 2FA on the registrar account if not already.
* Set up DNS in Cloudflare:
  * Proxied A/AAAA or CNAME placeholder (can be a 404 until landing page ships).
  * MX records for inbound mail (Fastmail, Google Workspace, or ImprovMX forwarding — decide inline).
  * SPF, DKIM, DMARC records matching mail provider.
* Configure at minimum `hello@rafn.dev` (forwarding or mailbox).

## Acceptance criteria

* `dig rafn.dev` resolves; WHOIS shows Voron Software GmbH as registrant (or privacy proxy with correct backing data).
* Sending a test email to `hello@rafn.dev` is delivered.
* DMARC policy set to at least `p=none` with reports going to a monitored address.

## Notes

* Budget: \~€15/yr for the domain itself.
* `rafn.com` is owned by a Seattle construction firm — not a target.
* This issue blocks the landing page and any package metadata that wants to point at a homepage URL.

title: Wire CLI commands (`bench`, `query`, `compare`, `trend`, `export`) to `Backend` trait
description:
## Goal

Wire all CLI commands to dispatch through the `Backend` trait so that `rafn bench`, `rafn query`, `rafn compare`, `rafn trend`, and `rafn export` work end-to-end against `.rafn/store/` when `backend.type = "local"` in `rafn.toml`.

This is the payoff ticket — after this, a user can `rafn init` and `rafn bench` with zero infrastructure.

## Key design decision: `rafn bench` is the primary entry point

`rafn bench` is a single command that executes the benchmark harness AND stores results via the active backend. No separate "upload" step. The name mirrors `cargo bench` / `go test -bench` — users already have the muscle memory. The two-step workflow (run externally → upload file) only makes sense for CI / cloud contexts and should not be the default local experience.

For the cloud backend, a separate `rafn push` command (future, out of scope) can handle shipping pre-existing output files to the API. The word "upload" is avoided — it implies a remote target.

## Command behavior under local backend

* `rafn bench`: detect harness, execute benchmarks, parse output, resolve commit SHA from git HEAD, write results to `.rafn/store/<sha>.pb`. Single command, zero friction.
* `rafn query --commit <sha>`: read from local store, display results in the same tabular format as cloud.
* `rafn compare --base <sha> --head <sha>`: read both commits from local store, compute and display regressions/improvements.
* `rafn trend --name <benchmark>`: walk git history, collect matching results from local store, display time-series.
* `rafn export`: export from local store in the same formats as cloud (JSON, CSV).

## Backend resolution

At CLI startup, resolve config ([VRN-93](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-93/repo-level-config-file-rafntoml-discovery-schema-precedence)) → instantiate the appropriate `Backend` impl → pass to command handlers. Commands should not contain any `if local / else cloud` branching — they receive a `Backend` and use it.

## Acceptance criteria

* `rafn bench` executes benchmarks and stores results in a single invocation
* All read commands work against local backend with no network calls
* Output format is identical to cloud backend (same table columns, same JSON schema for export)
* Helpful error messages when: no `rafn.toml` found, `.rafn/` directory missing, no data for requested commit
* Integration test: `init` → `bench` (with Criterion) → `query` → `compare` → `trend` full round-trip

project_milestone.name: M2 — Ingestion & storage (Rust)
project_milestone.description: End-to-end path from a developer's `cargo bench` run to persisted time-series data: cargo bench result parser, common internal benchmark format, ClickHouse schema, ingestion API, and client-side shipper (CLI + GitHub Action) for Rust projects.

title: `LocalBackend` implementation: `.rafn/store/` read/write with protobuf files
description:
## Goal

Implement `LocalBackend` — the `Backend` trait implementation ([VRN-95](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-95/backend-trait-abstraction-define-interface-wrap-existing-cloud-path)) that reads and writes benchmark data to `.rafn/store/` as binary protobuf files, using git for commit ordering.

## Storage format

### Directory layout

```
.rafn/
  store/
    <full-commit-sha>.pb
```

Flat directory, one file per commit SHA. No subdirectory sharding — at local scale (hundreds to low thousands of commits) this is fine. If a user hits filesystem limits, they should be on the cloud backend.

### File format

Each `.pb` file contains length-delimited `Benchmark` protobuf messages (using the standard protobuf length-prefix convention: varint length followed by message bytes). This supports appending multiple benchmark runs for the same commit without rewriting the file.

No separate index file. Queries that need commit ordering shell out to `git log` (or use `gix`/`git2`). Listing stored commits is a directory scan of `store/` filtered against git history.

### File header

First 4 bytes: magic `RAFN` (0x5241464E), followed by 1-byte version (0x01). This allows future format changes without ambiguity. Length-delimited messages follow immediately after the header.

## Backend trait operations

* **Write**: serialize benchmarks as length-delimited messages, append to `store/<sha>.pb` (create if new)
* **Query**: read and deserialize all messages from `store/<sha>.pb`
* **Compare**: read two `.pb` files, match benchmarks by name, compute deltas
* **List**: `readdir` on `store/`, filter to `.pb` files, extract SHAs
* **Trend**: for a given benchmark name, walk git history (`git log --format=%H`), read each commit's `.pb` file if it exists, extract matching benchmark records

## Acceptance criteria

* Implements all `Backend` trait methods against `.rafn/store/`
* Correctly handles: first write to a commit, appending to existing commit, missing commit file
* Uses git for commit ordering in trend queries (graceful fallback to file mtime if not in a git repo)
* File format includes magic + version header
* Round-trip test: write benchmarks, read them back, verify equality
* Integration test: write multiple commits, run trend query, verify ordering

project_milestone.name: M2 — Ingestion & storage (Rust)
project_milestone.description: End-to-end path from a developer's `cargo bench` run to persisted time-series data: cargo bench result parser, common internal benchmark format, ClickHouse schema, ingestion API, and client-side shipper (CLI + GitHub Action) for Rust projects.

title: `Backend` trait abstraction: define interface, wrap existing cloud path
description:
## Goal

Define a `Backend` trait that all CLI read/write commands dispatch through, so the same commands work transparently against local storage or the cloud API. The active backend is determined by `rafn.toml`'s `backend.type` field (from [VRN-93](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-93/repo-level-config-file-rafntoml-discovery-schema-precedence)).

## Trait surface

The trait should cover the operations needed by existing CLI commands:

* **Write**: store benchmark results for a commit
* **Query**: retrieve benchmark results for a commit
* **Compare**: retrieve results for two commits (base vs head)
* **List**: list commits that have stored benchmark data
* **Trend**: retrieve results for a benchmark name across N commits

Keep the trait minimal — these are the operations the CLI actually needs. Don't model the full ClickHouse query surface. The trait operates on domain types, not proto or HTTP types.

## Implementation scope

This ticket covers the trait definition and refactoring the existing cloud/API code path behind it (`CloudBackend`). The `LocalBackend` implementation is VRN-96.

## Acceptance criteria

* `Backend` trait defined in an appropriate crate (likely `rafn-core` or a new `rafn-backend`)
* Existing API client code wrapped in a `CloudBackend` struct implementing the trait
* CLI commands receive a `dyn Backend` / `impl Backend` based on resolved config
* Compiles and existing cloud path continues to work unchanged

project_milestone.name: M2 — Ingestion & storage (Rust)
project_milestone.description: End-to-end path from a developer's `cargo bench` run to persisted time-series data: cargo bench result parser, common internal benchmark format, ClickHouse schema, ingestion API, and client-side shipper (CLI + GitHub Action) for Rust projects.

title: `rafn init` command: scaffold config, `.rafn/` directory, `.gitignore` entry
description:
## Goal

`rafn init` scaffolds a repo for local benchmark tracking: creates `rafn.toml` with `backend.type = "local"`, creates the `.rafn/` storage directory, and appends `.rafn/` to `.gitignore`.

## Behavior

1. Check for existing `rafn.toml` in CWD — abort with a helpful message if one exists
2. Prompt-free: write `rafn.toml` with sensible defaults (backend type `local`, project name inferred from directory name or git remote)
3. Create `.rafn/store/` directory
4. If `.gitignore` exists and doesn't already contain `.rafn/`, append it. If no `.gitignore`, create one with `.rafn/`
5. Print a summary of what was created

## Output example

```
Created rafn.toml (backend: local)
Created .rafn/store/
Added .rafn/ to .gitignore

Run benchmarks and use `rafn upload` to store results locally.
```

## Acceptance criteria

* Creates valid `rafn.toml` with local backend config
* Creates `.rafn/store/` directory structure
* Handles `.gitignore` creation/append idempotently
* Refuses to overwrite existing `rafn.toml`
* Works in non-git directories (skip `.gitignore` step, warn)

project_milestone.name: M2 — Ingestion & storage (Rust)

title: Repo-level config file (`rafn.toml`): discovery, schema, precedence
description:
## Goal

Introduce a repo-level configuration file `rafn.toml` that lives alongside the project's source code, distinct from the existing user-level config at `~/.config/rafn/config.toml`. This is the prerequisite for all local-backend work and also the natural home for per-repo cloud settings.

## Config schema

```toml
[backend]
type = "local"   # "local" | "cloud", default "cloud"

[backend.cloud]
api_url = "https://api.rafn.dev"
# api_key always via RAFN_API_KEY env, never in file

[project]
name = "my-project"
```

TOML only — no YAML support for now. Single format keeps discovery and parsing simple.

## Discovery

Walk up from CWD toward filesystem root looking for `rafn.toml`. Stop at the first match. This is the same pattern used by `.gitignore`, `Cargo.toml`, `pyproject.toml`, etc.

## Precedence

Repo-level (`rafn.toml`) > user-level (`~/.config/rafn/config.toml`) > compiled defaults. Fields present in the repo config override the corresponding user-level fields; absent fields fall through.

## Acceptance criteria

* `rafn.toml` is discovered by walking up from CWD
* Config struct merges repo-level over user-level over defaults
* `backend.type` field parsed and validated (`local` | `cloud`)
* Existing user-level config continues to work unchanged when no `rafn.toml` is present
* Unit tests for discovery, merge, and validation

description: Implement partition pruning to skip irrelevant S3 partitions during query execution, reducing S3 API calls and improving performance.

title: Implement Basic Query Optimizer
description:
## Problem

The optimizer crate is currently an empty placeholder. Query optimization is essential for performance.

## Scope

Implement basic query optimization rules to replace the placeholder.

### Phase 1 Optimizations

* Predicate pushdown - Push filters closer to data source
* Projection pruning - Read only required columns
* Partition pruning - Skip irrelevant S3 partitions
* Join reordering - Optimize join order based on cardinality

### Phase 2 Optimizations (Future)

* Materialized view support
* Query result caching
* Cost-based optimization
* Advanced join strategies

## Implementation Details

* Build on DataFusion's optimizer framework
* Add KaonDB-specific rules for S3-native optimizations
* Focus on minimizing S3 API calls (major cost driver)

## Affected Files

* `optimizer/` - Main implementation
* `session/` - Integration with query planning
* `distributed/` - Distributed query optimizations

## Success Criteria

* TPC-H queries show measurable improvement
* S3 API call count reduced by 30%+
* Query latency improved by 20%+
* Optimization rules tested and validated

## Effort

\~2-3 weeks

title: Expand Integration Tests - 50+ SQL Test Cases
description:
## Problem

Currently only 1 SQL integration test exists (`select1.sql`). Need comprehensive test coverage for all SQL operations.

## Goal

Add 50+ SQL test cases to `integration-tests/tests/stateless/` directory.

## Test Coverage Areas

### Basic Operations

* CREATE TABLE (various data types, partition keys, sorting keys)
* DROP TABLE (existing, non-existent, error cases)
* INSERT INTO (single row, multiple rows, from SELECT)
* SELECT (simple, with WHERE, with JOIN, with aggregations)

### Data Types

* INT, BIGINT, SMALLINT
* FLOAT, DOUBLE, DECIMAL
* VARCHAR, TEXT
* DATE, TIMESTAMP
* BOOLEAN
* NULL handling

### Query Complexity

* WHERE clauses (AND, OR, NOT, IN, BETWEEN)
* ORDER BY (ASC, DESC, multiple columns)
* GROUP BY with aggregations (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX)
* JOINs (INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL OUTER)
* Subqueries (correlated, uncorrelated)
* LIMIT and OFFSET
* DISTINCT

### Edge Cases

* Empty tables
* Large result sets
* NULL values in various operations
* Division by zero
* Type mismatches
* Invalid SQL syntax

### Error Cases

* Table not found
* Column not found
* Type conversion errors
* Constraint violations

## Test Format

Follow existing pattern:

* `.sql` file with query
* `.out` file with expected output
* Use `test_each_file` framework

## Success Criteria

* 50+ test cases covering all major SQL operations
* All tests pass consistently
* Error cases properly validated
* Tests run in CI/CD

## Effort

\~1 week

title: Create TPC-H Benchmark Suite
description:
## Problem

## Goal

## Scope

### Benchmark Implementation

### CI/CD Integration

* Publish results as artifacts

### Reporting

* Chart performance trends over time
* Compare with previous versions

## Infrastructure

* Use [criterion.rs](<http://criterion.rs>) for benchmarking
* Setup dedicated benchmark environment
* Ensure consistent hardware for comparisons

## Success Criteria

* Performance results published

description:
## Problem

Metrics are exposed but no monitoring dashboard exists. Operators can't visualize system health.

## Scope

### Prometheus Setup

### Grafana Dashboards

#### Overview Dashboard

title: Develop Python Client Library
description:
## Problem

No client libraries exist. Users must make raw HTTP/gRPC calls. Python is the primary language for data engineers.

## Scope

Create a Python client library for KaonDB.

## Features

### Core Functionality

* Connection management
* Query execution
* Result fetching (pandas DataFrame, list of dicts)
* Async support (asyncio)
* Connection pooling
* Error handling
* Authentication support

### API Design

```python
from kaondb import KaonDB

# Synchronous API
db = KaonDB(url="http://localhost:8082", api_key="...")
result = db.query("SELECT * FROM table")
df = result.to_pandas()

# Async API
async with KaonDB(url="...") as db:
    result = await db.query("SELECT * FROM table")
```

### Additional Features

* Parameterized queries
* Batch query execution
* Streaming results for large datasets
* Schema introspection
* Type hints throughout

### Package Management

* PyPI package (`pip install kaondb`)
* Type stubs included
* Requirements: minimal dependencies
* Python 3.8+ support

### Testing

* Unit tests (pytest)
* Integration tests against live KaonDB
* Type checking (mypy)
* Code coverage >80%

## Repository

Create separate repo: `voron-software/kaondb-python`

## Success Criteria

* Clean pythonic API
* Published on PyPI
* Example notebooks provided

## Effort

\~2 weeks

title: Add High Availability for Metastore
description:
## Problem

Metastore is a single point of failure. Production deployments require HA for reliability.

## Scope

Implement high availability for the Metastore component with automatic failover.

## HA Architecture Options

### Option 1: Active-Passive with Leader Election (Recommended)

* Multiple metastore instances
* Leader election (etcd, Consul, or Raft)
* Active instance handles all requests
* Passive instances ready for failover
* Automatic failover on failure

### Option 2: Active-Active (Future)

* Multiple active instances
* Distributed consensus (Raft)
* Load balancing across instances

## Implementation (Option 1)

### Leader Election

* Integrate etcd or implement Raft
* Leader lease with heartbeat
* Automatic failover (<10s)
* Split-brain prevention

### Snapshot Replication

* Real-time snapshot sync to standby
* S3-based snapshot sharing
* Standby catches up on promotion

### Client Coordination

* Frontend/Worker detect leader changes
* Retry on failover
* Connection pooling updates

### Health Checks

* Leader health monitoring
* Automatic demotion on health failure
* External health check endpoint

## Testing

* Failover tests (kill leader)
* Split-brain scenarios
* Network partition tests
* Performance under HA

## Deployment

* Kubernetes StatefulSet (3 replicas)
* Service discovery integration
* Helm chart updates

## Success Criteria

* No single point of failure
* Failover <10 seconds
* Zero data loss on failover

## Effort

\~3-4 weeks

title: Personal Legal: Ehevertrag preparation
description: Umbrella ticket covering the personal legal steps needed to protect assets in connection with the UG formation: obtain legal expense insurance coverage (ÖRAG Deckungszusage) and sign the Ehevertrag (modifizierte Zugewinngemeinschaft).

title: UG Formation (Gründung)
description:
Umbrella ticket covering all steps required to legally incorporate the UG (haftungsbeschränkt) in Germany and complete post-incorporation registrations.

Sequential critical path: virtual office → notary → bank account → Stammkapital deposit → Handelsregister → Finanzamt → VBG / DRV Bund.

title: Finanzamt registration (steuerliche Erfassung)
description:
## Goal

Register the UG with the Finanzamt (steuerliche Erfassung) to obtain the Steuernummer. Required for invoicing and for Stripe KYC.

## Scope

* Complete the Fragebogen zur steuerlichen Erfassung (via ELSTER or paper)
* Declare business activities: SaaS, open-source, licensed software, consulting
* Clarify VAT registration (Regelbesteuerung expected given B2B SaaS)
* Register for OSS (One-Stop-Shop) if EU cross-border B2C sales are anticipated
* Steuerberater should handle or review the filing

## Output

Steuernummer (and USt-IdNr. if applicable) — required for Stripe account KYC verification.

title: Handelsregister registration
description:
## Goal

Complete the Handelsregister (commercial register) entry for the UG. This is the step that brings the legal entity into existence.

## Scope

* Notary submits the filing on your behalf (standard process with Musterprotokoll)
* Confirm receipt of Handelsregisternummer (HRB number)
* Verify the entry is publicly visible in the Handelsregister ([handelsregister.de](<http://handelsregister.de>))

## Output

HRB number required for Finanzamt registration, VBG registration, and DRV Bund filing.

title: Deposit Stammkapital
description:
## Goal

Deposit the full Stammkapital into the Commerzbank account and obtain proof of deposit for the Handelsregister filing.

## Scope

* Transfer Stammkapital amount to the new business account
* Obtain bank confirmation letter (Einzahlungsbestätigung) — required by the notary / Handelsregister
* Forward confirmation to notary to proceed with Handelsregister filing

title: Open Commerzbank business account
description:
## Goal

Open the primary business bank account at Commerzbank (branch-based onboarding).

## Scope

* Book an in-branch appointment with the notarized Gründungsdokument
* Commerzbank is selected as primary: Vollbank, branch onboarding, human escalation path, English portal/app support
* Account will be used for Stammkapital deposit and all operating transactions
* A secondary account for retained earnings above deposit guarantee thresholds should be evaluated in parallel (not blocking)

title: Notary appointment — UG formation (Musterprotokoll)
description:
## Goal

Attend the notary appointment to execute the UG formation deed. Musterprotokoll is appropriate given the solo structure.

## Scope

* Use Musterprotokoll (§ 2 Abs. 1a GmbHG) — no custom Gesellschaftsvertrag needed
* Stammkapital: \~€1,000–1,500
* Single Gesellschafter and Geschäftsführer
* Registered address: virtual office ([VRN-63](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-63/set-up-virtual-office-address-in-berlin))
* Bring valid ID and virtual office address confirmation

## Output

Notarized Gründungsdokument required for Commerzbank account opening and Handelsregister filing.

title: Sign Ehevertrag (modifizierte Zugewinngemeinschaft)
description:
## Goal

Sign the Ehevertrag **before** the UG notary appointment. With the UG not yet in existence, its value is near zero (Stammkapital only), which minimizes the Geschäftswert used to calculate notary fees.

## Scope

* Engage Fachanwalt für Familienrecht with international private law experience (EU Regulation 2016/1103 — cross-border asset element)
* Target structure: modifizierte Zugewinngemeinschaft, carving UG shares out of Zugewinnausgleich
* Notarize the Ehevertrag
* Submit ÖRAG reimbursement claim after completion

## Constraint

Must be completed before the UG formation notary appointment.

title: Obtain ÖRAG Deckungszusage for Ehevertrag
description:
## Goal

Obtain written Deckungszusage from ÖRAG before engaging a Fachanwalt für Familienrecht for the Ehevertrag. This minimizes out-of-pocket exposure.

## Scope

* Contact ÖRAG under §2k ARB (Erweiterter Beratungs-Rechtsschutz im Familien-, Lebenspartnerschafts- und Erbrecht)
* Request written coverage confirmation (Deckungszusage) for Ehevertrag drafting and notarization
* Note: cross-border property element (EU Regulation 2016/1103) requires Fachanwalt with international private law experience — confirm coverage applies
* Expected net reimbursement after deductible: \~€700

title: Engage Steuerberater for UG formation
description:
## Goal

Engage a Steuerberater before any formal formation steps. This is a prerequisite for the notary appointment and all subsequent registration steps.

## Scope

* Confirm Musterprotokoll suitability for a solo, single-activity-phase UG
* Validate Stammkapital target (\~€1,000–1,500)
* Clarify tax structure: minimal or no GF salary, retained earnings strategy
* Confirm Finanzamt registration sequence and OSS applicability for SaaS
* Establish DATEV handoff expectations for ongoing bookkeeping

title: Stripe webhook handler
description:
## Goal

Receive Stripe webhook events, verify their authenticity, and write subscription state transitions to the ClickHouse transitions table ([VRN-54](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-54/clickhouse-billing-state-schema-transitions-table-dictionary)).

## Scope

### Webhook endpoint

* `POST /api/webhooks/stripe` — unauthenticated (Stripe calls this), verified via `stripe-signature` header + webhook signing secret
* Raw body must be preserved for signature verification (no middleware that parses/consumes the body before verification)

### Event handling

Handle these Stripe events at minimum:

* `checkout.session.completed` — initial subscription activation
* `customer.subscription.updated` — plan changes, renewals, status transitions
* `customer.subscription.deleted` — cancellation finalized
* `invoice.payment_failed` — flag the subscription as `past_due`

Each event handler extracts the relevant fields and inserts a row into the `subscription_transitions` table. The `stripe_event_id` in the `ReplacingMergeTree` provides idempotency.

### Tenant resolution

* Resolve `tenant_id` from `stripe_customer_id` using Stripe Customer metadata (populated during checkout in [VRN-55](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-55/stripe-checkout-session-flow)) or a reverse lookup in the transitions table
* Log and reject events for unknown customers

## Acceptance criteria

* Webhook signature verification works with test-mode signing secret
* All four event types produce correct transition rows in ClickHouse
* Duplicate event delivery does not produce duplicate state (ReplacingMergeTree dedup)
* Unknown/unhandled event types are acknowledged (200) but not processed

title: ClickHouse billing state schema: transitions table + dictionary
description:
## Goal

Create the ClickHouse schema for persisting subscription billing state: an append-only transitions table for the full audit log, and a Dictionary for O(1) current-state lookups from the auth middleware.

## Design

### Transitions table

`ReplacingMergeTree` keyed on `(tenant_id, stripe_event_id)` with version = event timestamp. This handles Stripe webhook redeliveries without application-layer dedup.

Columns (minimum):

* `tenant_id UUID`
* `stripe_customer_id String`
* `stripe_subscription_id String`
* `stripe_event_id String` — idempotency key
* `status LowCardinality(String)` — e.g. `active`, `past_due`, `canceled`, `trialing`, `unpaid`
* `event_type LowCardinality(String)` — the Stripe event type that triggered this transition
* `event_time DateTime64(3)` — Stripe event timestamp
* `ingested_at DateTime64(3) DEFAULT now64(3)`
* `metadata String DEFAULT ''` — optional JSON blob for extra context

### Dictionary

Source query resolves latest state per tenant via `argMax(status, event_time)` grouped by `tenant_id`. Keyed on `tenant_id`, exposes `status`, `stripe_customer_id`, `stripe_subscription_id`.

`LIFETIME(MIN 0 MAX 30)` — 30-second staleness window is acceptable for billing gating.

Layout: `HASHED()` (tenant count will be small for a long time).

## Acceptance criteria

* Migration file created following the existing `migrations/` convention
* `schema/schema.sql` regenerated via `scripts/dump-clickhouse-schema.sh`
* Dictionary is queryable: `SELECT dictGet('subscription_state', 'status', tenant_id)` returns the latest status
* CI schema-drift check passes

description:

description:

title: GitHub Action: `rafn-dev/action` wrapping CLI upload for CI
description:
GitHub Action that wraps `rafn upload` for CI pipelines. Users add this to their workflow after `cargo bench`:

```yaml
- uses: rafn-dev/action@v1
  with:
    criterion-dir: target/criterion
```

The action:

* Installs the `rafn` CLI binary (via cargo-binstall or direct download)
* Authenticates via OIDC federation (`id-token: write`) with API key fallback (`RAFN_API_KEY` secret)
* Runs `rafn upload` with the provided options
* Passes through git context from `$GITHUB_SHA`, `$GITHUB_REF`, etc.

Acceptance criteria:

* Action installs and runs `rafn upload` successfully in a GitHub Actions workflow
* OIDC federation works with `id-token: write` permission
* Falls back to API key if OIDC isn't configured
* Action published to GitHub Marketplace (or at least usable via repo reference)

title: Implement Rafn ClickHouse schemas (metadata + benchmark data)
description:
## Goal

Umbrella ticket grouping all ClickHouse schema implementation work for Rafn.

## Scope

ClickHouse holds two distinct categories of data, tracked in sub-issues:

* **Metadata schema** (tenants, users, projects, api_tokens) — [VRN-12](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-12/clickhouse-schema-v0-metadata-tables-tenants-users-projects-api-tokens)
* **Benchmark data schema** (raw samples + daily rollup with AggregatingMergeTree) — [VRN-24](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-24/implement-clickhouse-schema-raw-samples-daily-rollup-with)

This parent exists so the schema work has a single home; the children stay separately scoped because they target different tables, different access patterns, and arrive at different milestones.

## Acceptance criteria

* All sub-issues completed.
* Schema migrations live in a single, repeatable migration tool (introduced in [VRN-12](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-12/clickhouse-schema-v0-metadata-tables-tenants-users-projects-api-tokens), reused by [VRN-24](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-24/implement-clickhouse-schema-raw-samples-daily-rollup-with)).

title: Set up BSR-generated Rust SDK consumption in uploader repo
description:
## Context

The .proto schema definitions live in a private Git repository and are pushed to the public BSR module at `buf.build/voron-software/rafn`. The public uploader repo needs to consume Rust bindings generated from these schemas via BSR's Cargo registry.

## BSR Rust SDK Details

BSR generates Rust crates using the `community/neoeinstein-prost` and `community/neoeinstein-tonic` plugins. These are protoc plugin wrappers around the canonical `prost` and `tonic` crates — not forks. The generated code is standard prost/tonic output.

**Uploader likely only needs prost (message serialization), not tonic (gRPC services).** Evaluate whether tonic is needed based on the uploader's transport design.

## Tasks

### Private .proto repo (CI)

- [ ] Add GitHub Actions workflow: `buf-setup-action` → `buf-lint-action` → `buf-breaking-action` → `buf push`
- [ ] Configure `buf-breaking-action` against `main` branch to catch wire-incompatible changes before they reach BSR
- [ ] Store `BUF_API_TOKEN` as GitHub Actions secret

### Public uploader repo (Cargo integration)

- [ ] Add `.cargo/config.toml` with Buf registry:

  ```toml
  [registries.buf]
  index = "sparse+https://buf.build/gen/cargo/"
  credential-provider = "cargo:token"
  ```
- [ ] Add BSR crate dependency: `cargo add --registry buf voron-software_rafn_community_neoeinstein-prost`
- [ ] If gRPC needed: `cargo add --registry buf voron-software_rafn_community_neoeinstein-tonic`
- [ ] Document in README that `buf registry login` is needed before first build (writes token to credential store)
- [ ] Verify generated types compile and match expected schema

### Version pinning strategy

- [ ] Pin to a specific BSR label (e.g., `stable`) rather than auto-tracking `main` for the uploader's release builds
- [ ] Consider using Dependabot or Renovate to propose SDK version bumps as PRs

## Known Risks

* **Plugin maintenance:** `neoeinstein-prost`/`neoeinstein-tonic` are community-maintained (\~192 stars, active as of late 2025). Not owned by Buf or prost/tonic teams. If maintenance lapses, fallback is `build.rs` + `prost-build` + `tonic-build` with local `buf generate` — generated types are identical.
* **Version lag:** Plugin updates trail upstream prost/tonic releases. Pin plugin versions in `buf.gen.yaml` to avoid surprises.
* **BSR output structure coupling:** BSR cargo registry expects specific file layout from plugin. Breaking changes between plugin versions (e.g., 0.4→0.5) have required `flat_output_dir` workarounds.

## References

* BSR module: [https://buf.build/voron-software/rafn](<https://buf.build/voron-software/rafn>)
* BSR Cargo docs: [https://buf.build/docs/bsr/generated-sdks/cargo/](<https://buf.build/docs/bsr/generated-sdks/cargo/>)
* Plugin repo: [https://github.com/neoeinstein/protoc-gen-prost](<https://github.com/neoeinstein/protoc-gen-prost>)
* Buf GitHub Actions: [https://buf.build/docs/bsr/ci-cd/gh-actions/](<https://buf.build/docs/bsr/ci-cd/gh-actions/>)

title: Implement ClickHouse schema: raw samples + daily rollup with AggregatingMergeTree
description:
## Summary

Implement the ClickHouse schema for benchmark data storage: a raw high-resolution table with TTL, a daily rollup using `AggregatingMergeTree`, and a materialized view to bridge them.

## Design

### Raw table: `benchmark_samples`

Stores every individual measurement. Partitioned by `toYYYYMM(ts)`, TTL 90 days. Primary key prefix is `(tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch, ts)`.

Key columns:

* `tenant_id UUID` — multi-tenant isolation
* `project_id UUID`, `benchmark_id String` — benchmark identity
* `branch LowCardinality(String)`, `commit_sha FixedString(40)` — SCM context
* `run_id UUID` — groups samples from a single invocation
* `runner_profile FixedString(32)` — opaque hash of hardware/env parameters, **not** in ORDER BY; use bloom_filter skip index instead
* `value Float64`, `unit LowCardinality(String)` — the measurement
* `metadata Map(String, String)` — extensible context (OS, CPU, toolchain)

### Rollup table: `benchmark_daily`

Lives forever. Partitioned by `toYear(day)`. `AggregatingMergeTree` engine.

ORDER BY: `(tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch, day)` — runner_profile excluded, bloom filter skip index used instead.

Aggregate columns:

* `pcts AggregateFunction(quantiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99), Float64)` — single-pass interior quantiles, returns `Array(Float64)` on merge
* `val_min / val_max` — exact boundaries via `min`/`max` (t-digest drifts at extremes)
* `val_avg / val_stddev` — for z-score regression detection
* `sample_count` — observation count
* `commits AggregateFunction(groupUniqArray(64), FixedString(40))` — clamped list of up to 64 unique commit SHAs per day-bucket

### Materialized view: `benchmark_daily_mv`

Glue layer. Transforms inserts into `benchmark_samples` into aggregated rows in `benchmark_daily` using `-State` combinators: `quantilesState`, `minState`, `maxState`, `avgState`, `stddevPopState`, `countState`, `groupUniqArrayState(64)`.

## Design decisions

* **Partition by time only** — no tenant_id in partition key. With 100+ tenants × months, composite partitions exceed the recommended <1000 limit. Tenant filtering relies on sparse index via ORDER BY prefix.
* **runner_profile as opaque hash** — equivalence logic lives in application layer. High cardinality expected. Bloom filter skip index on both tables for efficient filtering.
* **quantiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)** — single column, single pass. No 0.0/1.0 levels; exact min/max columns cover boundaries.
* **groupUniqArray(64)** — capped at 64 unique SHAs per day. Bounds storage (\~2.6 KB worst case) while preserving commit traceability for regression bisection.
* **stddevPop** — enables z-score anomaly detection against historical baselines without touching raw data.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Raw table DDL executes on ClickHouse Cloud
- [ ] Rollup table DDL executes on ClickHouse Cloud
- [ ] MV DDL executes and correctly links raw → rollup
- [ ] Insert test data into raw table, verify rollup populates via MV
- [ ] Query rollup with `-Merge` combinators, verify correct quantile array, min, max, avg, stddev, count, and commit list
- [ ] Bloom filter index on runner_profile verified via EXPLAIN
- [ ] TTL on raw table verified (data expires after 90d)

## SQL reference

```sql
CREATE TABLE benchmark_samples
(
    tenant_id      UUID,
    project_id     UUID,
    benchmark_id   String,
    branch         LowCardinality(String),
    commit_sha     FixedString(40),
    run_id         UUID,
    runner_profile FixedString(32),
    ts             DateTime64(3),
    value          Float64,
    unit           LowCardinality(String),
    metadata       Map(String, String),
    INDEX idx_runner runner_profile TYPE bloom_filter GRANULARITY 4
)
ENGINE = MergeTree()
PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(ts)
ORDER BY (tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch, ts)
TTL ts + INTERVAL 90 DAY;

CREATE TABLE benchmark_daily
(
    tenant_id       UUID,
    project_id      UUID,
    benchmark_id    String,
    branch          LowCardinality(String),
    runner_profile  FixedString(32),
    unit            LowCardinality(String),
    day             Date,
    pcts            AggregateFunction(quantiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99), Float64),
    val_min         AggregateFunction(min, Float64),
    val_max         AggregateFunction(max, Float64),
    val_avg         AggregateFunction(avg, Float64),
    val_stddev      AggregateFunction(stddevPop, Float64),
    sample_count    AggregateFunction(count),
    commits         AggregateFunction(groupUniqArray(64), FixedString(40)),
    INDEX idx_runner runner_profile TYPE bloom_filter GRANULARITY 4
)
ENGINE = AggregatingMergeTree()
PARTITION BY toYear(day)
ORDER BY (tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch, day);

CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW benchmark_daily_mv
TO benchmark_daily
AS SELECT
    tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch,
    runner_profile, unit,
    toDate(ts)                                        AS day,
    quantilesState(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)(value)            AS pcts,
    minState(value)                                   AS val_min,
    maxState(value)                                   AS val_max,
    avgState(value)                                   AS val_avg,
    stddevPopState(value)                             AS val_stddev,
    countState()                                      AS sample_count,
    groupUniqArrayState(64)(commit_sha)               AS commits
FROM benchmark_samples
GROUP BY tenant_id, project_id, benchmark_id, branch,
         runner_profile, unit, day;
```

title: Provision Zitadel Cloud (EU) instance for staging; document self-host migration path
description:
## Goal

Provision a Zitadel Cloud instance in the EU region for non-local environments (staging first, prod when we're ready to onboard external users). This is the Phase 0 managed-hosting path for Zitadel; self-hosting is deferred to M4+ (see ADR).

Local dev continues to use the containerized Zitadel from [VRN-9](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-9/local-dev-environment-docker-compose-with-clickhouse-zitadel-just) — this ticket does not touch local dev.

## Scope

### Account & instance

* Create a Zitadel Cloud account under a dedicated work email (not a personal one)
* Region: **EU** (data residency for Voron Software GmbH)
* Create an instance: `rafn-staging`
* Custom domain: defer until we have a `rafn.dev` DNS footprint — use the Zitadel-provided subdomain initially
* Enable 2FA on the root account; store recovery codes in 1Password (or whichever secret store Voron uses)

### Bootstrap identical to local

Run `cargo run -p rafn-xtask -- zitadel-bootstrap --base-url <staging-url> --sa-key <path>` against the staging instance. The xtask (built per [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications)) must produce the same org/project/app structure it does locally:

* `rafn-system` org + `rafn-platform` project + three applications (`rafn-web`, `rafn-cli`, `rafn-api`)
* The `tenant_id` Action
* **No dev users** — staging starts empty and we invite real team members

Staging-specific deltas:

* Redirect URIs for `rafn-web` point at the staging frontend URL (not `localhost:3000`)
* Email delivery configured (Zitadel Cloud provides SMTP; verify sender domain is set up)
* Password policy stricter than dev (min length 12, require MFA for admins)

### Secrets management

* Service-account key (`rafn-staging-admin-sa.json`) is **never** committed — store in 1Password / GitHub Actions secrets / whichever secret backend we standardize on
* Document in `docs/auth.md` where the key lives and how to rotate it
* CI can fetch the key from a secret backend to re-run the bootstrap xtask when config drifts

### Upgrade path documentation

Capture in `docs/auth.md`:

* Current: Zitadel Cloud EU, managed by Zitadel AG
* Future: self-hosted Zitadel on our own k8s/Postgres, same codebase (AGPL-3.0)
* What to migrate when we flip: user data (export via API), org/project config (re-run xtask against the new instance), DNS/issuer URL cutover plan
* Signals that would trigger the migration (cost, compliance, control)

## Out of scope

* Actually migrating to self-hosted — that's an M4+ ticket when the signal triggers
* Production instance (`rafn-prod`) — separate ticket when we're onboarding paying customers
* SAML IdP setup for enterprise customers — separate ticket in the \~12-month horizon
* Custom-domain email setup — defer

## Acceptance criteria

* `https://<rafn-staging>.zitadel.cloud/.well-known/openid-configuration` returns 200 with the expected issuer
* `cargo run -p rafn-xtask -- zitadel-bootstrap --base-url <staging-url>` succeeds and is idempotent against the staging instance
* The staging instance's project, applications, and Action match the dev config (diff via xtask dry-run)
* The admin SA key is in the secret store, not on disk or in git
* `docs/auth.md` has a "Staging & production auth" section with the access URL, where to find the SA key, and the self-host migration path
* The root Zitadel Cloud account has 2FA enabled and recovery codes stored

## Notes for agents

* This ticket touches a paid/metered external service. Before provisioning, confirm we're within the free tier or that the billing arrangement is approved.
* EU region is non-negotiable — if Zitadel Cloud doesn't offer EU-resident data for the selected plan, raise it and halt, don't default to US.
* The staging instance is *not* a playground — treat it with the same config-as-code discipline as prod. Manual UI tweaks get overwritten by the next xtask run.
* When rotating the SA key, always create the new one, update the secret store, test, then revoke the old one — never the reverse order.
