title: Config & secrets: layered config (defaults + env files + env vars), dev/ci/staging/prod
description:
## Goal

One coherent configuration story across dev, CI, staging, prod. No secrets in the repo. No environment-specific code branches scattered through the codebase.

## Scope

### Crate: `crates/rafn-config`

* Library crate; used by `rafn-api` and `rafn-xtask`
* Depends on `config` crate (Rust `config` = `"0.14"`) or `figment` — pick one, document choice
* Exposes a single `RafnConfig` struct with `serde::Deserialize`

### Config layering (highest precedence last)

1. Built-in defaults (compiled into binary)
2. `config/default.toml` — checked in, non-secret defaults
3. `config/{env}.toml` — one per env (`dev`, `ci`, `staging`, `prod`); checked in, non-secret
4. Environment variables prefixed `RAFN_` (e.g. `RAFN_CLICKHOUSE__URL`; double underscore = nested key)
5. `.env` file at repo root in dev only (gitignored)

### `RafnConfig` shape (initial)

```rust
pub struct RafnConfig {
    pub server: ServerConfig,         // bind_addr, port, shutdown_timeout
    pub clickhouse: ClickHouseConfig, // url, user, password, database
    pub auth: AuthConfig,             // oidc_issuer, audience, jwks_cache_ttl
    pub telemetry: TelemetryConfig,   // log_format, log_level, otlp_endpoint (optional)
}
```

All fields deserialized; validation at load time with clear errors ("missing `RAFN_CLICKHOUSE__URL`" rather than "invalid config").

### Secrets handling

* **No secrets committed, ever.** Secret values must come from env vars in staging/prod
* `config/dev.toml` may contain dev-only secrets (e.g. ClickHouse password `default`) because they match the compose stack and have no value outside dev
* `.env.example` committed at repo root listing every `RAFN_*` variable the app reads, with placeholder values
* `rafn-api` logs a startup banner with resolved config — **with secrets redacted** (passwords shown as `[REDACTED]`, token values masked). Implement via a `Debug` impl that doesn't leak.

### Env selection

* `RAFN_ENV` selects which `config/{env}.toml` to overlay. Default: `dev`.
* `rafn-api` prints the active env on startup.

### Tests

* Unit tests in `rafn-config`:
  * Defaults load successfully
  * Env var overrides apply correctly
  * Missing required field produces a helpful error
  * Redaction hides secret fields in `Debug` output

## Out of scope

* Secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, Vault) — M4 production concern
* Hot-reload of config — not needed
* Per-tenant feature flags — M3+ if ever

## Acceptance criteria

* `cargo run -p rafn-api` with no env set picks up `config/default.toml` + `config/dev.toml`
* Setting `RAFN_SERVER__PORT=9999` overrides the port
* `.env.example` exists, is complete, and is referenced from `docs/dev-setup.md`
* A deliberately wrong config (e.g. invalid CH URL) fails on startup with a clear message, not a panic deep in the request path
* `grep -r -E "(password|secret|token)" config/ infra/` reveals nothing that would be dangerous to leak (dev-only values only)

## Notes for agents

* Prefer `config` crate unless there's a specific reason for `figment`; both work. Document the choice in `crates/rafn-config/README.md`.
* The `Debug` redaction is easy to get wrong — test it. A leaked password in logs is a real incident.
* `.env` parsing: use `dotenvy`, not the unmaintained `dotenv`

title: ClickHouse schema v0: metadata tables (tenants, users, projects, api_tokens) + migration tool
description:
## Goal

Define the v0 metadata schema in ClickHouse and a repeatable migration mechanism. This schema is the tenant/project/token backbone that the API will read and write on every request.

## Context & trade-off (read this)

ClickHouse is being used for metadata in M1 as a deliberate scope-reduction — it avoids introducing a second datastore now. The cost: CH has no enforced uniqueness, no foreign keys, weak update semantics, and `ReplacingMergeTree` gives eventual consistency on dedup. We accept this for M1 and plan to revisit in M3/M4 when user-visible CRUD gets heavy. This ticket must document the constraint explicitly in `crates/rafn-storage/README.md`.

## Scope

### Tables (all in a `metadata` database, separate from future `bench` database)

All tables use `ReplacingMergeTree(updated_at)` unless noted. All have `tenant_id String` as the first column of the sorting key where applicable. All timestamps are `DateTime64(3, 'UTC')`.

1. `tenants`
   * `tenant_id String` (ULID)
   * `slug String` (url-safe, unique by convention) — must match the Zitadel org slug/domain for the same tenant (see [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications)); this is the contract between Rafn's metadata and the `tenant_id` claim injected by the Zitadel Action
   * `display_name String`
   * `created_at DateTime64(3, 'UTC')`
   * `updated_at DateTime64(3, 'UTC')`
   * `deleted_at Nullable(DateTime64(3, 'UTC'))`
   * `ORDER BY tenant_id`
2. `users` (shadow of Zitadel; populated on first login)
   * `user_id String` — Zitadel `sub` claim
   * `tenant_id String`
   * `email String`
   * `display_name String`
   * `role LowCardinality(String)` — `admin` | `member`
   * `created_at`, `updated_at`, `deleted_at`
   * `ORDER BY (tenant_id, user_id)`
3. `projects`
   * `project_id String` (ULID)
   * `tenant_id String`
   * `slug String`
   * `display_name String`
   * `language LowCardinality(String)` — `rust` | `python` | `java` | `other`
   * `created_at`, `updated_at`, `deleted_at`
   * `ORDER BY (tenant_id, project_id)`
4. `api_tokens` (for CI / CLI auth; distinct from user OIDC sessions)
   * `token_id String` (ULID)
   * `tenant_id String`
   * `project_id Nullable(String)` — null = tenant-wide
   * `name String`
   * `token_hash FixedString(64)` — SHA-256 hex of secret; plaintext never stored
   * `created_by String` — `user_id`
   * `scopes Array(LowCardinality(String))` — e.g. `['ingest', 'read']`
   * `created_at`, `last_used_at Nullable`, `expires_at Nullable`, `revoked_at Nullable`
   * `ORDER BY (tenant_id, token_id)`

### Migration tool

* Use `refinery` with a ClickHouse driver, or a hand-rolled versioned SQL runner in `crates/rafn-storage/src/migrations/`
* Migrations live as numbered SQL files: `crates/rafn-storage/migrations/0001_metadata_init.sql`, etc.
* `rafn-xtask migrate` subcommand applies pending migrations
* Track applied migrations in a `_migrations` table in the `metadata` DB

### Query helpers

In `rafn-storage`:

* `MetadataClient` struct wrapping the `clickhouse` crate's client
* Typed accessors: `get_tenant_by_slug`, `list_projects_for_tenant`, `find_token_by_hash`
* All queries take `tenant_id` explicitly as a parameter — no implicit context. Tenant scoping is enforced at the query-builder layer.

### Uniqueness caveats (document prominently)

* `tenants.slug`, `projects.slug`, `api_tokens.token_hash` are logically unique but **not enforced by CH**
* Application layer must do read-before-write checks for these; race conditions are possible
* Mitigation for tokens: 256-bit random + SHA-256 makes collision practically impossible; slug collision is caught by app-layer check and surfaced as 409
* `tenants.slug` uniqueness is doubly important because it's the key we use to correlate with the Zitadel org — a collision would mean two Rafn tenants mapped to one Zitadel org. The app-layer check must cover this on tenant creation.

## Out of scope

* Benchmark sample tables (`runs`, `samples`) — M2
* Row policies for tenant isolation — evaluate in M3 when query surface is real
* Migration rollback — M1 accepts forward-only; document as limitation

## Acceptance criteria

* `rafn-xtask migrate` applied to a fresh ClickHouse creates all 4 tables + `_migrations`
* Re-running `migrate` is idempotent (no duplicate applications)
* `MetadataClient` has passing integration tests against a real CH container that cover insert, update-via-replacement, and tenant-scoped list
* `crates/rafn-storage/README.md` documents the CH-as-metadata-store trade-off and the migration path to Postgres
* Schema file is reviewable — no more than \~200 lines of SQL total

## Notes for agents

* Do not add `runs` or `samples` tables — that's M2 scope
* Use ULIDs (not UUIDs) for human-readable IDs — there's a `ulid` crate
* `ReplacingMergeTree` deduplicates asynchronously; queries that must see the latest row need `FINAL` or explicit `argMax` — pick one convention and document it
* The `users.user_id` is Zitadel's `sub` claim (a Zitadel-generated identifier). Do not attempt to make it human-readable or match it to `email` — users can change email, `sub` is stable.

title: Cargo workspace scaffolding: crate layout, shared deps, shared lints
description:
## Goal

Establish the Cargo workspace crate layout that the rest of M1+ will populate. This ticket is structural: it creates empty (or near-empty) crates with clear responsibilities and shared lint/dependency configuration, so subsequent tickets drop code into known homes instead of inventing layout.

Note: repo and workspace root already exist; this ticket extends what's there, it does not start from scratch.

## Scope

### Workspace layout

```
rafn/
├── Cargo.toml              # [workspace] root with members, shared deps, shared lints
├── rust-toolchain.toml
├── clippy.toml
├── rustfmt.toml
├── crates/
│   ├── rafn-api/           # HTTP API service (axum) — main binary
│   ├── rafn-core/          # domain types, errors, IDs (no I/O)
│   ├── rafn-auth/          # OIDC verification, tenant/principal extraction
│   ├── rafn-storage/       # ClickHouse client wrapper, schema migrations
│   ├── rafn-config/        # layered config loading
│   └── rafn-xtask/         # dev tasks (seed, migrate, etc.) — not published
├── infra/                  # compose files, Keycloak realm, CH config
├── docs/
└── scripts/
```

### Root `Cargo.toml`

* `[workspace] resolver = "3"` (or `"2"` if toolchain pin predates resolver 3; document choice)
* `[workspace.package]` with shared `version`, `edition = "2021"`, `license`, `repository`
* `[workspace.dependencies]` — pin versions once for: `tokio`, `axum`, `serde`, `serde_json`, `thiserror`, `tracing`, `tracing-subscriber`, `clickhouse` (the `clickhouse` crate), `jsonwebtoken`, `reqwest`, `uuid`, `time`, `anyhow`
* Member crates use `dep = { workspace = true }`
* `[workspace.lints]` with shared clippy config (see below)

### Shared lints (`[workspace.lints.clippy]`)

Enable at `warn` or `deny`:

* `pedantic` (warn)
* `unwrap_used` (deny)
* `expect_used` (deny in non-test)
* `panic` (deny in non-test)
* `todo` (warn)
* `dbg_macro` (deny)

Allow (to reduce noise):

* `module_name_repetitions`
* `missing_errors_doc` (for M1)

### Per-crate stubs

Each crate gets:

* `Cargo.toml` inheriting workspace settings
* `src/lib.rs` (or `src/main.rs` for `rafn-api` and `rafn-xtask`) with a minimal `#![deny(unsafe_code)]` and a placeholder doc comment
* One trivial unit test so `cargo test` has something to run and the crate compiles

### Tooling config

* `rustfmt.toml`: `edition = "2021"`, `max_width = 100`, `group_imports = "StdExternalCrate"`, `imports_granularity = "Crate"` (nightly-only — document if using stable-compatible subset)
* `clippy.toml`: cognitive complexity threshold, msrv declaration
* `.editorconfig` at repo root

## Out of scope

* UI crate (`rafn-web` with Leptos) — M3
* Benchmark parser crates (`rafn-cargo-bench-parser`, etc.) — M2
* Public SDK crate — M2+

## Acceptance criteria

* `cargo build --workspace` succeeds from clean checkout
* `cargo test --workspace` succeeds (placeholder tests pass)
* `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` is green
* Adding `dbg!()` to any non-test file triggers a clippy error locally
* Each crate has a one-paragraph `README.md` stating its responsibility and explicit non-responsibilities

## Notes for agents

* Do **not** add `rafn-web` (Leptos) in this ticket — it's an M3 concern and has different tooling (trunk, wasm target)
* Keep dependencies minimal in stubs; resist adding a dep "we'll probably need" — add it in the ticket that actually uses it
* `rafn-core` must have **no** I/O dependencies (no tokio, no reqwest, no clickhouse). If something needs I/O, it belongs in a different crate.

title: Ship placeholder landing page at rafn.dev (Cloudflare Pages)
description:
## Goal

Ship a minimal one-page site at `https://rafn.dev` so the domain resolves to something credible (not a parked placeholder), captures early interest, and links back to branded email.

## Scope

### Content

* Single static page with:
  * Raven logo + wordmark
  * One-line value prop (e.g., "Performance regression tracking for your benchmarks")
  * 2–3 sentence elaboration targeting the Rust/cargo bench audience first
  * "Coming soon" or "Private beta" signal
  * Waitlist / interest form: email capture only (no account creation)
  * Footer: copyright Voron Software GmbH, `hello@rafn.dev`, links to GitHub org (if public) and to imprint/privacy (required under German law — see below)

### Legal pages (hard requirement for a DE company)

* `/impressum` — Impressum per §5 TMG (company name, address, managing director, commercial register number, VAT ID, contact).
* `/datenschutz` (or `/privacy`) — GDPR-compliant privacy notice covering the waitlist email collection, hosting (Cloudflare), and any analytics.

### Tech

* Static site, hand-rolled HTML+CSS or a minimal SSG (Astro, Zola, or plain HTML — pick the lightest option).
* No client-side framework unless already justified.
* Email capture: serverless endpoint (Cloudflare Worker) writing to KV or a third-party service (Buttondown, Mailchimp — pick one, note GDPR implications).
* Deploy: **Cloudflare Pages**, connected to a GitHub repo in the Voron Software org.
* Set up `rafn.dev` and `www.rafn.dev` (with redirect) in Cloudflare Pages; HTTPS automatic.
* Basic SEO: `<title>`, meta description, OG tags using the 1200×630 image from [VRN-7](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-7/consolidate-brand-assets-logos-typography-color-tokens).
* Lighthouse: Performance ≥ 95, Accessibility ≥ 95.

## Acceptance criteria

* `https://rafn.dev` returns a 200 with the landing page; `http://rafn.dev` and `www.rafn.dev` redirect correctly.
* Impressum and privacy pages are reachable and compliant.
* Email submission reaches our inbox or mailing list.
* OG card renders correctly in Slack, LinkedIn, and Twitter/X previews.
* Repo is in the Voron Software org, CI/CD auto-deploys on merge to `main`.

## Dependencies

* Blocked by: [VRN-5](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-5/acquire-rafndev-domain) (need the domain) and [VRN-7](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-7/consolidate-brand-assets-logos-typography-color-tokens) (need finalized logo + OG image + tokens).

## Notes

* Keep the page intentionally small — every extra element is something to maintain before we have a product.
* Consider disabling Cloudflare Web Analytics or using a privacy-friendly analytics solution to keep the GDPR story simple.

title: Consolidate brand assets (logos, typography, color tokens)
description:
## Goal

Finalize the Rafn brand asset set so both the landing page and the downstream product UI (Leptos, M3) pull from a single consistent source of truth.

## Scope

### Logos

* Master raven logo in SVG, clean paths, named layers.
* Export variants:
  * Full logo (wordmark + raven)
  * Icon-only (raven)
  * Horizontal and stacked layouts
* File formats per variant: SVG, PNG @1x/@2x/@3x, favicon (16, 32, 48, 180 for apple-touch-icon), ICO.
* Light and dark backgrounds (two color treatments minimum).
* Social preview image (OG image, 1200×630) for rafn.dev.

### Typography

* Pick one primary (UI/body) and optionally one display font.
* Prefer variable fonts with open licensing (e.g., Inter, IBM Plex, JetBrains Mono for code).
* Document weights in use.

### Color tokens

* Define primary/secondary/accent, neutral scale, semantic colors (success/warning/error/info).
* Deliver as both CSS custom properties and a JSON token file (for later consumption by Leptos theming).

### Deliverable

* A `brand/` directory in the monorepo (or a dedicated `rafn-brand` repo if the monorepo doesn't exist yet — decide as part of M1) containing:
  * `logos/` with all exports
  * `fonts/` or a reference to the font source + license
  * `tokens.json` and `tokens.css`
  * `README.md` with usage guidelines (clearspace, minimum size, don'ts)

## Acceptance criteria

* All assets exist in the brand directory and render correctly in both light and dark mode.
* `tokens.json` can be consumed programmatically.
* A short visual identity guide (can be a single markdown doc) documents logo usage and the color/type system.

## Notes

* Raven logos are already prepared per existing brand constraints — this ticket is about packaging and standardization, not re-design.
* Blocks: [VRN-7](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-7/consolidate-brand-assets-logos-typography-color-tokens) (landing page) and the M3 Leptos UI shell.

title: Create Examples Repository
description:
## Problem

No example applications exist. Users don't know how to use KaonDB for real-world scenarios.

## Scope

Create repository with 5+ example applications demonstrating KaonDB usage.

## Repository

`voron-software/kaondb-examples`

## Example Applications

### 1\. Real-Time Analytics Dashboard

* Streaming data ingestion (Kafka/WebSocket)
* Aggregation queries
* Web dashboard (React/Vue)
* Time-series visualization

### 2\. Log Aggregation Pipeline

* Log ingestion from multiple sources
* Structured logging format
* Query patterns for log analysis
* Search and filtering UI

### 3\. Time-Series Data Analysis

* IoT sensor data ingestion
* Time-bucketed aggregations
* Anomaly detection queries
* Visualization with charts

### 4\. ETL Workflow Integration

* Extract data from external sources
* Transform using SQL
* Load into KaonDB
* Airflow/Prefect integration

### 5\. Data Warehouse Replacement

* Migration from traditional RDBMS
* Star schema implementation
* OLAP query patterns
* BI tool integration (Metabase)

## Each Example Includes

* Complete source code
* Docker Compose setup
* Sample data generators
* README with setup instructions

## Success Criteria

* 5 working examples
* All examples run with single command
* Clear documentation
* Realistic use cases

## Effort

title: Publish TPC-H Benchmark Results
description:
## Problem

No performance comparisons exist. Users don't know how KaonDB performs vs competitors.

## Dependencies

* Requires: TPC-H Benchmark Suite (#227)

## Scope

Run comprehensive benchmarks and publish results comparing KaonDB to competitors.

## Benchmark Plan

### Systems to Compare

* KaonDB
* ClickHouse
* DuckDB
* PostgreSQL (baseline)

### Test Configurations

* TPC-H Scale Factor 1 (1GB)
* TPC-H Scale Factor 10 (10GB)
* All 22 TPC-H queries
* Identical hardware (AWS c5.4xlarge)
* Identical S3 storage (where applicable)

### Metrics to Measure

* Query execution time (per query)
* Total benchmark time
* Peak memory usage
* S3 API call count
* S3 data transfer volume
* Cost per query (compute + storage)

### Performance Characteristics

* Scaling behavior (1 → 10 → 100 workers)
* Concurrent query performance
* Cold cache vs warm cache
* Impact of data partitioning

## Results Publication

### Documentation

* Add `docs/benchmarks.md`
* Include all benchmark results
* Reproducible instructions
* Hardware specifications

## Success Criteria

* Fair, reproducible benchmarks
* Published results with methodology
* Honest discussion of strengths/weaknesses
* Data available for verification

## Effort

\~1 week (after benchmark suite ready)

title: Implement Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
description:
## Problem

No authorization system exists. All authenticated users have full access. Enterprise deployments require role-based permissions.

## Dependencies

* Requires: Authentication Layer (#223)

## Scope

Implement RBAC to control user permissions at database, schema, and table levels.

## Permission Model

### Roles

* **Admin** - Full system access
* **Developer** - Create/modify schemas and tables
* **Analyst** - Read-only query access
* **Writer** - Insert/update data
* Custom roles

### Permissions

* Database-level: CREATE, DROP
* Schema-level: CREATE, DROP, ALTER
* Table-level: SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE, DROP
* System-level: MANAGE_USERS, MANAGE_ROLES

### Grant/Revoke

```sql
GRANT SELECT ON database.schema.table TO role;
REVOKE INSERT ON database.schema.table FROM role;
GRANT role TO user;
```

## Implementation

### Storage

* Role definitions in metastore
* User-role mappings
* Permission grants
* Efficient permission checking

### Enforcement

* Query planning phase checks
* SQL parser integration
* gRPC middleware authorization
* HTTP endpoint authorization

### Management

* SQL commands (GRANT, REVOKE)
* CLI commands for user/role management
* API for programmatic access

## Security

* Principle of least privilege
* Audit logging of permission changes
* Permission inheritance
* Default deny policy

## Success Criteria

* Fine-grained access control working
* Performance impact <10ms per query
* Comprehensive audit trail

## Effort

\~2-3 weeks

title: Implement Multi-Tenancy Support
description:
## Problem

Single-user system only. Cannot isolate multiple customers/teams in shared KaonDB deployment.

## Scope

Add multi-tenancy with tenant isolation for data and resources.

## Tenancy Model

### Tenant Isolation

* Database-level isolation (each tenant gets database)
* Schema-level isolation (each tenant gets schema)
* Namespace-based isolation
* Resource quotas per tenant

### Tenant Management

* Create/delete tenants
* Tenant configuration
* Tenant-specific settings
* Tenant usage tracking

## Resource Isolation

### Storage

* Separate S3 prefixes per tenant
* Storage quotas
* Cost allocation

### Compute

* CPU quotas
* Memory quotas
* Query concurrency limits
* Query timeout limits

### Metadata

* Tenant-specific snapshots
* Isolated catalog namespaces
* Cross-tenant query prevention

## Authentication Integration

* Tenant identification (subdomain, header, API key prefix)
* Tenant-scoped users
* SSO with tenant mapping

## Monitoring

* Per-tenant metrics
* Resource usage dashboards
* Cost allocation reports
* Quota enforcement alerts

## Success Criteria

* Complete tenant data isolation
* Resource quotas enforced
* No cross-tenant data leakage
* Performance overhead <5%

## Effort

\~3-4 weeks

title: Add Chaos Engineering Tests
description:
## Problem

No failure injection testing exists. System resilience to failures is unknown.

## Scope

Implement chaos engineering tests to validate KaonDB's resilience to various failure scenarios.

## Test Framework

* Choose framework (Chaos Mesh, Toxiproxy, custom scripts)
* Integration with test suite
* Automated chaos tests in CI (optional)
* Manual chaos testing procedures

## Failure Scenarios

### Service Failures

* Kill Frontend - Mid-query kill
* Kill Worker - During query execution
* Kill Metastore - During metadata operation
* Kill Writer - During data ingestion
* Multiple simultaneous service failures

### Network Failures

* Network partition - Split cluster
* Network latency - Inject 100ms, 500ms, 1s delays
* Packet loss - 10%, 50% packet loss
* Connection reset - TCP connection failures
* DNS failures - Service discovery issues

### Storage Failures

* S3 unavailable - Temporary S3 outage
* S3 throttling - Rate limit errors (429)
* S3 slow - High latency responses
* Partial S3 failure - Some objects inaccessible
* Data corruption - Invalid data in S3

### Resource Exhaustion

* Memory pressure - Limit available memory
* CPU starvation - Limit CPU allocation
* File descriptor limit - Exhaust FDs
* Connection pool exhaustion - Max connections

### Cascading Failures

* Retry storm - Thundering herd
* Slow query cascade - One slow query blocks others
* Dependency failure propagation

## Validation Criteria

### For Each Scenario

* System behavior documented
* Recovery time measured
* Data integrity verified (no data loss)
* Error messages clear and actionable
* Automatic recovery (where possible)
* Manual recovery procedures

### Success Metrics

* No data loss in any scenario
* Graceful degradation
* Clear error messages to users
* Recovery time <60 seconds for common failures
* No permanent state corruption

## Implementation

* Create chaos test suite (`integration-tests/chaos/`)
* Docker Compose chaos scenarios
* Kubernetes chaos scenarios

## Effort

\~2 weeks

title: Subscription access gating middleware
description:
## Goal

Gate API and UI access based on subscription status, using the ClickHouse dictionary ([VRN-54](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-54/clickhouse-billing-state-schema-transitions-table-dictionary)) for low-latency lookups.

## Scope

### Axum middleware / extractor

* Create a `SubscriptionState` extractor (or middleware layer) that reads the current subscription status for the request's `tenant_id` via `dictGet('subscription_state', 'status', tenant_id)`
* Runs after the Zitadel auth extractor (needs `tenant_id` from the JWT)

### Access tiers

Define and enforce these tiers:

* `active` **/** `trialing` → full access (read + write)
* `past_due` → full access with a UI banner warning about payment failure (grace period)
* `canceled` → read-only access for a configurable grace period after cancellation, then blocked
* **No subscription record** → free tier (if applicable) or onboarding flow redirect

### Bypass paths

* Webhook endpoint (`/api/webhooks/stripe`) must not be gated
* Health/readiness endpoints must not be gated
* Public/unauthenticated routes (landing page, docs) are unaffected

### Fallback for dictionary miss

* If the dictionary returns no result for a `tenant_id` (new tenant, dictionary not yet refreshed), fall back to a direct query on the transitions table
* Log dictionary misses for observability

## Acceptance criteria

* Requests with `active` subscription proceed normally
* Requests with `canceled` subscription beyond grace period receive 403 with a clear message
* `past_due` requests succeed but response includes a header or field indicating payment issue
* Unauthenticated/public routes are not affected by gating

title: Stripe Checkout session flow
description:
## Goal

Implement the Stripe Checkout flow so that an authenticated user can subscribe to Rafn from within the app.

## Scope

### Stripe Customer mapping

* On first checkout, create a Stripe Customer and persist the `stripe_customer_id` → `tenant_id` mapping
* If a Stripe Customer already exists for the tenant, reuse it
* Populate Stripe Customer metadata with `tenant_id` for traceability

### Checkout session endpoint

* `POST /api/billing/checkout` — creates a Stripe Checkout Session in `subscription` mode
* Uses the Price ID from the Stripe product configuration ([VRN-53](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-53/stripe-account-setup-and-product-configuration))
* Sets `success_url` and `cancel_url` pointing back to the Rafn UI
* Requires authenticated request; extracts `tenant_id` from the Zitadel JWT

### Post-checkout UX

* Success redirect page shows an "activating…" state until the webhook-driven dictionary update propagates (up to 30s)
* Cancel redirect returns to the pricing/billing page without side effects

## Dependencies

* [VRN-53](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-53/stripe-account-setup-and-product-configuration) (Stripe account & product config) — need test-mode keys and Price ID
* Zitadel auth flow must be functional (tenant_id in JWT)

## Notes

* `async-stripe` crate is the pragmatic choice for Stripe API interactions
* The redirect is NOT the source of truth for activation — the webhook handler (VRN-56) is

title: Rafn CLI binary distribution
description:
## Goal

Umbrella ticket for all work related to distributing pre-built Rafn CLI binaries to end users and CI pipelines.

## Structure

* **cargo-binstall support** — enables `cargo binstall rafn` and cross-compiled release artifacts — [VRN-36](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-36/add-cargo-binstall-support-for-rafn-cli)
* **taiki-e/install-action support** — enables one-liner CLI install in GitHub Actions workflows — [VRN-37](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-37/support-taiki-einstall-action-for-rafn-cli)

## Sequencing

[VRN-36](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-36/add-cargo-binstall-support-for-rafn-cli) (cargo-binstall) must land first; [VRN-37](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-37/support-taiki-einstall-action-for-rafn-cli) (install-action) verifies and extends the fallback path established by [VRN-36](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-36/add-cargo-binstall-support-for-rafn-cli).

## Acceptance criteria

* All sub-issues completed.
* Users can install the Rafn CLI on Linux, macOS, and Windows without compiling from source.
* CI pipelines can install the CLI via `taiki-e/install-action`.

title: Add cargo-binstall support for Rafn CLI
description:
## Goal

Enable `cargo binstall rafn` to install pre-built Rafn CLI binaries instead of compiling from source.

## Why

cargo-binstall is the standard mechanism for distributing pre-built Rust CLI tools. It eliminates multi-minute compile times for users and is the foundation for `taiki-e/install-action` fallback support in CI.

## Implementation

### 1\. Cargo.toml metadata

Add to the CLI crate's `Cargo.toml`:

```toml
[package.metadata.binstall]
pkg-url = "{ repo }/releases/download/v{ version }/rafn-{ target }{ archive-suffix }"
pkg-fmt = "tgz"

[package.metadata.binstall.overrides.x86_64-pc-windows-msvc]
pkg-fmt = "zip"
```

### 2\. Release CI workflow

Create `.github/workflows/release.yml` triggered on `v*` tags that:

* Cross-compiles for target triples: `x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu`, `aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu`, `x86_64-apple-darwin`, `aarch64-apple-darwin`, `x86_64-pc-windows-msvc`
* Packages each binary as `rafn-{target}.tar.gz` (`.zip` for Windows)
* Binary must be at archive root, named `rafn` / `rafn.exe`
* Uploads archives as GitHub Release assets via `softprops/action-gh-release`

### 3\. Publish to [crates.io](<http://crates.io>)

cargo-binstall requires the crate to exist on [crates.io](<http://crates.io>) for metadata lookup. Ensure the CLI crate is published before tagging the first release.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `[package.metadata.binstall]` configured in CLI crate Cargo.toml
- [ ] Release workflow builds and uploads binaries for all 5 targets
- [ ] Tagging `v0.x.y` produces a GitHub Release with correctly named assets
- [ ] `cargo binstall rafn` installs successfully on Linux, macOS, and Windows

title: Procure Elestio managed ClickHouse instance
description:
## Objective

Set up a managed ClickHouse instance via Elestio for Rafn's prototype/development environment.

## Steps

1. Sign up at [elest.io](<http://elest.io>)
2. Deploy ClickHouseS3 service on Hetzner (cheapest option, \~$14/month)
3. Select EU region closest to other infra (e.g. `nbg1` or `fsn1`)
4. Configure firewall — restrict access to known IPs only
5. Verify connectivity from the application layer
6. Document connection details and credentials securely

## Notes

* Elestio provides: dedicated VM, automated backups, SSL, OS/software updates, monitoring
* Elestio does NOT provide: ClickHouse-specific tuning, ingestion pipelines, replication, or usage-based scaling
* This is the primary ClickHouse hosting path unless CH Cloud credits come through (see related ticket)

title: Corpus: C# — Fork MessagePack-CSharp, add GH Actions benchmark workflow
description:
## Project

**MessagePack-CSharp/MessagePack-CSharp** — The fastest MessagePack serializer for C#, 6.4K GitHub stars, 204M+ NuGet downloads. Performance is the project's core value proposition, so benchmarks are well-maintained and central to the codebase.

## Why this project (over dotnet/performance)

* Self-contained `benchmark/` directory with a ready-to-run BenchmarkDotNet project
* Covers serialization/deserialization across multiple strategies (IntKey, StringKey, Typeless) and competing libraries
* Single `dotnet run` to execute — finishes in minutes, not hours
* No scoping or filtering needed unlike dotnet/performance which benchmarks the entire .NET runtime
* Influential enough for demos: widely used in game dev, microservices, distributed systems

## Tasks

1. Fork `github.com/MessagePack-CSharp/MessagePack-CSharp` to Voron org
2. Create `.github/workflows/rafn-bench.yml`:
   * Trigger: `schedule` (nightly) + `workflow_dispatch`
   * Runner: `ubuntu-latest`
   * Steps: checkout, setup .NET SDK, `dotnet run -c Release` in `benchmark/SerializerBenchmark/` with JSON exporter enabled
   * Upload BenchmarkDotNet JSON results via Rafn CLI
3. Verify BenchmarkDotNet JSON output is parseable by Rafn's C#/BDN ingestion parser
4. Document: .NET SDK version, expected runtime, any NuGet restore quirks
5. Set up upstream sync

## Acceptance criteria

* Nightly workflow runs BenchmarkDotNet benchmarks from the `benchmark/` directory
* Results uploaded to Rafn and visible in dashboard
* Upstream sync mechanism documented

title: Corpus: Java — Fork FasterXML/jackson-benchmarks, add GH Actions workflow
description:
## Project

**FasterXML/jackson-benchmarks** — Dedicated JMH benchmark repository for Jackson, the most widely used Java JSON library. Maintained since Jackson 2.4 to track serialization/deserialization performance across releases.

## Why this project

* Purpose-built JMH benchmark repo (not benchmarks buried in a larger project)
* Covers read/write for multiple formats (JSON, Smile, CBOR) and multiple APIs (Streaming, Databind, Afterburner)
* Produces standard JMH JSON output — the canonical Java benchmark format
* Self-contained Maven project, straightforward to build and run

## Tasks

1. Fork `github.com/FasterXML/jackson-benchmarks` to Voron org
2. Create `.github/workflows/rafn-bench.yml`:
   * Trigger: `schedule` (nightly) + `workflow_dispatch`
   * Runner: `ubuntu-latest`
   * Steps: checkout, setup JDK 17, `mvn clean install`, `java -jar target/microbenchmarks.jar -rf json -rff results.json`
   * Upload `results.json` via Rafn CLI
3. Verify JMH JSON output is parseable by Rafn's JMH ingestion parser
4. Document: JDK version, Maven settings, benchmark filter patterns, expected runtime
5. Set up upstream sync

## Acceptance criteria

* Nightly workflow runs JMH benchmarks and exports JSON results
* Results uploaded to Rafn and visible in dashboard
* Upstream sync mechanism documented

title: Corpus: Go — Fork prometheus/prometheus, add GH Actions benchmark workflow
description:
## Project

**prometheus/prometheus** — The canonical monitoring system. TSDB and PromQL packages have solid `testing.B` benchmark coverage, and performance regressions are actively tracked by the community.

## Why this project

* Extensive `go test -bench` coverage across TSDB (storage, compaction, querying) and PromQL (evaluation, parsing)
* Performance-critical infrastructure project where regressions matter
* Better benchmark density than Tempo for microbenchmark corpus purposes
* Standard Go tooling (`testing.B`), no exotic dependencies

## Tasks

1. Fork `github.com/prometheus/prometheus` to Voron org
2. Create `.github/workflows/rafn-bench.yml`:
   * Trigger: `schedule` (nightly) + `workflow_dispatch`
   * Runner: `ubuntu-latest`
   * Steps: checkout, setup Go, `go test -bench=. -benchmem -count=5 ./tsdb/... ./promql/...`
   * Pipe output through Rafn CLI for upload
3. Verify Go bench output (`BenchmarkXxx   N   ns/op   B/op   allocs/op`) is parseable by Rafn's Go ingestion parser
4. Document: Go version requirements, any build tags, expected runtime, which packages to bench
5. Set up upstream sync

## Acceptance criteria

* Nightly workflow runs Go benchmarks for TSDB + PromQL packages
* Results uploaded to Rafn and visible in dashboard
* Upstream sync mechanism documented

title: Corpus: Rust — Fork BurntSushi/regex, add GH Actions benchmark workflow
description:
## Project

**BurntSushi/regex** — Rust's de facto regex engine with one of the most extensive Criterion benchmark suites in the ecosystem.

## Why this project

* Dozens of Criterion benchmarks across pattern types (literal, alternation, character classes) and input sizes
* `cargo-benchcmp` and `critcmp` were literally built against this project's output
* Actively maintained, performance-sensitive codebase
* Reasonable build/bench time on GH Actions runners

## Tasks

1. Fork `github.com/BurntSushi/regex` to Voron org
2. Create `.github/workflows/rafn-bench.yml`:
   * Trigger: `schedule` (nightly) + `workflow_dispatch`
   * Runner: `ubuntu-latest`
   * Steps: checkout, install Rust stable, `cargo bench -- --output-format=criterion`
   * Upload results via Rafn CLI
3. Verify Criterion JSON output is parseable by Rafn's Rust/Criterion ingestion parser
4. Document: required Rust toolchain version, any feature flags needed, expected bench runtime
5. Set up upstream sync (periodic rebase from upstream main)

## Acceptance criteria

* Nightly GH Actions workflow runs `cargo bench` successfully
* Benchmark results are uploaded to Rafn and visible in dashboard
* Upstream sync mechanism documented

title: ADR-0001: document identity provider decision (Zitadel)
description:
## Goal

Capture the identity-provider decision as a committed ADR so future contributors and agents understand why Zitadel was chosen, what was considered, and what would trigger a re-evaluation.

## Scope

Create `docs/adr/0001-identity-provider.md` with the following sections:

### Status

Accepted, 2026-04.

### Context

Rafn is a multi-tenant B2B SaaS (benchmark tracking) with thousands of projects, primary backend in Rust, EU-based (Voron Software GmbH). Phase 0 requires authentication and multi-tenancy as foundational, not deferred. SAML SSO for enterprise customers is expected within \~12 months. Managed hosting preferred for Phase 0, with a credible path to self-host later for cost predictability and data sovereignty.

### Decision

**Zitadel**, deployed as Zitadel Cloud (EU) for staging/prod during Phase 0, with local development using containerized Zitadel from docker-compose. Migration to self-hosted Zitadel is planned for M4+ if/when signals trigger it.

### Alternatives considered

* **Auth0** — rejected on cost; pricing escalates rapidly for B2B with multiple SSO connections.
* **Keycloak** — rejected on operational overhead (finicky config, JBoss-era ergonomics, weaker multi-tenancy).
* **WorkOS (AuthKit)** — strong B2B fit and generous free tier, but no self-host path. Migration away later means ripping out auth entirely. Per-connection SSO pricing at the \~12-month SAML horizon also outweighs savings vs. Zitadel self-hosted.
* **FusionAuth** — viable self-hosted alternative, but organizations/tenancy are weaker abstractions than Zitadel's. We'd own more tenant-model code.
* **Clerk** — per-MAU pricing wrong for B2B economics (one large customer = real cost); MAO cap bites early.
* **Ory (Kratos + Hydra + Keto)** — more assembly than Keycloak config. Contradicts the "not finicky" goal.

### Rationale

Three things combined:

1. Multi-tenancy is first-class (Instance > Organization > Project > Application) and maps cleanly to Rafn's tenant-per-customer model.
2. Zitadel Cloud and self-hosted run the same codebase, so "managed now, self-host later" is a config change, not a rewrite.
3. Standards-compliant OIDC + SAML (certified) means Rust integration via `openidconnect` / `jsonwebtoken` without a provider-specific SDK.

### Consequences

* We depend on Zitadel AG as a Series A vendor for staging/prod during Phase 0. Risk is bounded by AGPL-3.0: if the company fails, we keep running the same binary, self-hosted.
* No first-party Rust SDK; we use standard OIDC crates. Acceptable because Zitadel is strictly standards-compliant.
* AGPL-3.0 applies to modifications. Running unmodified Zitadel as auth infra does not trigger copyleft obligations. If we ever fork, modifications must be published.
* `users.user_id` in our metadata DB is Zitadel's `sub` claim, creating a soft coupling. Mitigated by keeping our `tenant_id` derived from the org **slug/domain** (our domain concept) rather than Zitadel's internal org ID.

### Revisit triggers

Re-evaluate the decision if any of the following occur:

* Zitadel Cloud EU pricing crosses a predefined cost threshold (define in `docs/auth.md`)
* Zitadel AG is acquired and the new owner changes license, pricing, or roadmap in a way that harms us
* We discover a functional limitation (e.g., SAML behavior, custom-claim shape, rate limits) that blocks an important feature and can't be worked around
* Our multi-tenancy model diverges from the org-per-customer pattern significantly enough that Zitadel's abstractions become friction

### Links

* [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications) — Zitadel instance configuration
* [VRN-9](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-9/local-dev-environment-docker-compose-with-clickhouse-zitadel-just) — Local dev environment with Zitadel
* [VRN-14](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-14/api-framework-skeleton-axum-service-with-oidc-auth-tenant-extraction) — API framework with OIDC auth
* [VRN-16](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-16/provision-zitadel-cloud-eu-instance-for-staging-document-self-host) — Zitadel Cloud (EU) staging provisioning

## Acceptance criteria

* `docs/adr/0001-identity-provider.md` exists and contains the sections above
* The ADR is linked from `docs/auth.md` (once that file exists per [VRN-13](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-13/zitadel-instance-configuration-projects-organizations-applications))
* The ADR is committed on the same branch as the first Zitadel-related implementation ticket so reviewers see context alongside code

## Out of scope

* A general ADR template or process definition — if we decide to adopt Architecture Decision Records more broadly, that's a separate ticket. For now this is a one-off.
* Any implementation work — this ticket produces a document, nothing else.

## Notes for agents

* Keep the ADR tight — under \~400 lines. Exhaustive is not the goal; capturing decision context for a future reader is.
* Do not restate anything that belongs in `docs/auth.md` (architecture specifics, how-to-login). ADR is decision + rationale; `auth.md` is operational.
* Prefer plain prose over bulleted lists where prose reads better. Bullets are appropriate for alternatives and triggers; rationale should be narrative.

title: Publish Rafn CLI to winget
description:
## Goal

Enable `winget install VoronSoftware.Rafn` as an installation path for the Rafn CLI on Windows.

## Context

The CLI already ships pre-built `x86_64-pc-windows-msvc` artifacts via GitHub Releases (used by `cargo-binstall`). winget requires a YAML manifest submitted to the `microsoft/winget-pkgs` community repository.

## What needs to happen

### Initial manifest submission

* Create multifile winget manifest under `manifests/v/VoronSoftware/Rafn/<version>/`:
  * `VoronSoftware.Rafn.yaml` (version)
  * `VoronSoftware.Rafn.installer.yaml` (installer URL, SHA256, architecture `x64`, installer type `zip`/`portable`)
  * `VoronSoftware.Rafn.locale.en-US.yaml` (description, license `Apache-2.0`, tags, homepage `rafn.dev`)
* Validate locally with `winget validate`
* Test in Windows Sandbox using `Tools/SandboxTest.ps1`
* Submit PR to `microsoft/winget-pkgs`, agree to Microsoft CLA

### CI automation for future releases

* Add a `wingetcreate update` step to the GitHub Actions release workflow that:
  * Computes SHA256 of the Windows `.zip` artifact
  * Generates an updated manifest
  * Opens a PR to `microsoft/winget-pkgs` automatically
* Requires a GitHub PAT with `public_repo` scope stored as a repository secret

## Installer strategy

Use **portable** installer type (standalone `.exe` in `.zip`). This aligns with the existing `cargo-binstall` artifact layout and avoids maintaining a separate Windows installer (Inno/WiX). winget will handle PATH management for portable packages.

## Acceptance criteria

* `winget search Rafn` returns `VoronSoftware.Rafn`
* `winget install VoronSoftware.Rafn` installs the CLI and `rafn` is available on PATH
* New releases automatically trigger a manifest update PR to `winget-pkgs`

title: Support taiki-e/install-action for Rafn CLI
description:
## Goal

Enable Rafn CLI installation in CI via `taiki-e/install-action`, the standard GitHub Action for installing Rust toolchain binaries.

## Why

Users setting up Rafn in their CI pipelines need a one-liner to install the CLI. `install-action` is the de facto standard — used by most Rust projects for tool installation in GitHub Actions. Supporting it means users can write:

```yaml
- uses: taiki-e/install-action@v2
  with:
    tool: rafn
```

## How it works

`install-action` resolves tools in order: built-in manifest → cargo-binstall fallback → cargo install. Once [VRN-36](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-36/add-cargo-binstall-support-for-rafn-cli) (cargo-binstall support) is complete, the fallback path works automatically with no additional configuration.

## Implementation

### Phase 1: Verify fallback path (immediate)

After [VRN-36](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-36/add-cargo-binstall-support-for-rafn-cli) is merged and a release is tagged:

* Verify `install-action` successfully installs via the binstall fallback
* Add an integration test workflow that uses `install-action` to install Rafn and runs a smoke command (e.g. `rafn --version`)

### Phase 2: Submit manifest entry (optional, polish)

For faster installs (skips binstall resolution), submit a PR to [taiki-e/install-action](<https://github.com/taiki-e/install-action/tree/main/manifests>) adding `rafn.json`:

```json
{
  "repository": "voron-software/rafn",
  "bin": ["rafn"],
  "signing": null,
  "asset_name": {
    "x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu": "rafn-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz",
    "aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu": "rafn-aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz",
    "x86_64-apple-darwin": "rafn-x86_64-apple-darwin.tar.gz",
    "aarch64-apple-darwin": "rafn-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz",
    "x86_64-pc-windows-msvc": "rafn-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
  }
}
```

This is only worth doing once Rafn has public users — taiki-e maintains quality bar on manifest entries.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `taiki-e/install-action` installs Rafn CLI successfully via binstall fallback
- [ ] Smoke test workflow validates the install path in CI
- [ ] (Optional) Manifest PR submitted once Rafn reaches public availability

title: rafn bisect: automated regression bisection via git bisect integration
description:
## Summary

Implement `rafn bisect`, a CLI command that automates performance regression bisection. Given a "good" commit and a "bad" commit, it runs benchmarks on both endpoints to establish baseline vs regressed performance, then launches `git bisect run` with a Rafn-powered script that determines pass/fail for each intermediate commit based on benchmark results.

This turns a manual, hours-long regression hunt into a single command.

## Motivation

When a performance regression is detected between two releases or commits, developers currently must manually bisect or guess which commit introduced it. `git bisect` already provides the binary search mechanics, but it requires a script that can answer "good or bad?" for each commit. Rafn already knows how to run benchmarks and detect regressions — this feature connects those two capabilities.

## User-facing behavior

```
rafn bisect --good <commit> --bad <commit> [--benchmark <filter>] [--threshold <pct>]
```

### Phase 1: Baseline establishment

1. Check out the `--good` commit, run the selected benchmarks, store results as the **baseline**.
2. Check out the `--bad` commit, run the same benchmarks, verify that a regression is actually present relative to baseline.
3. If no regression is detected between good and bad, exit early with a clear message — there is nothing to bisect.

### Phase 2: Automated bisect

4. Invoke `git bisect start <bad> <good>`.
5. Invoke `git bisect run rafn bisect-step --baseline <stored-baseline-ref>` (or equivalent internal subcommand).
6. For each commit `git bisect` checks out, the `bisect-step` subcommand:
   * Builds the project (respecting the same build profile used for benchmarks).
   * Runs the filtered benchmark suite.
   * Compares results against the stored baseline using Rafn's regression detection logic.
   * Exits with code **0** (good) if performance is within threshold, or **1** (bad) if a regression is detected.
   * Exits with code **125** (skip) if the commit fails to build or benchmarks cannot run.
7. After `git bisect` converges, display the first-bad commit with a summary of the regression magnitude.

### Phase 3: Cleanup

8. Run `git bisect reset` to restore the original HEAD.
9. Print a final report: first-bad commit hash, author, message, and the performance delta vs baseline.

## CLI options

| Flag | Required | Default | Description |
| -- | -- | -- | -- |
| `--good` | yes | — | Commit SHA or ref where performance is acceptable |
| `--bad` | yes | — | Commit SHA or ref where regression is present |
| `--benchmark` | no | all | Filter expression to select which benchmarks to run |
| `--threshold` | no | from config | Percentage change that constitutes a regression |
| `--runs` | no | from config | Number of benchmark iterations per commit (for statistical confidence) |
| `--build-cmd` | no | auto-detect | Custom build command if default detection is insufficient |

## Technical considerations

* **Working tree cleanliness**: The command must refuse to start if the git working tree has uncommitted changes, since `git bisect` will check out commits.
* **Build failures**: Commits that fail to compile must return exit code 125 so `git bisect` skips them rather than treating them as good or bad.
* **Statistical confidence**: Each bisect step should run benchmarks multiple times (configurable via `--runs`) and apply the same statistical regression detection Rafn uses elsewhere (e.g., comparing distributions, not single samples).
* **Benchmark filter consistency**: The exact same benchmark filter must be used for baseline and every bisect step. Store this alongside the baseline reference.
* **Storage of baseline**: Baseline results should be stored in a temporary location (e.g., `.rafn/bisect/`) that is cleaned up on completion or can be resumed if interrupted.
* **Interruption recovery**: If the process is interrupted (Ctrl+C, crash), the user should be able to run `rafn bisect --reset` to clean up the git bisect state and temporary data.
* **Cross-platform**: Must work on Linux and macOS. Windows support is stretch.

## Internal subcommand: `rafn bisect-step`

This is not user-facing in normal usage but is the script passed to `git bisect run`. It must:

* Accept a reference to the stored baseline (path or identifier).
* Build the project.
* Run benchmarks.
* Compare against baseline.
* Return the correct exit code (0 / 1 / 125).

Consider whether this should be a hidden subcommand or an internal mechanism (e.g., the main `rafn bisect` command writes a small shell/batch wrapper that calls back into `rafn`).

## Exit codes

| Code | Meaning |
| -- | -- |
| 0 | Bisect completed, first-bad commit identified |
| 1 | No regression detected between good and bad (nothing to bisect) |
| 2 | Git working tree is dirty |
| 3 | Bisect was interrupted or failed |

## Out of scope (for now)

* Multi-metric bisection (bisecting on more than one benchmark simultaneously with different thresholds).
* Remote benchmark execution (running benchmarks on a different machine).
* Integration with Rafn SaaS for storing/viewing bisect history.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `rafn bisect --good <sha> --bad <sha>` correctly identifies the first commit that introduced a regression in a test repository with a known-bad commit.
- [ ] Build failures at intermediate commits are skipped (exit 125), not misclassified.
- [ ] If no regression exists between good and bad, the command exits cleanly with a clear message.
- [ ] `rafn bisect --reset` cleans up git bisect state and temporary files.
- [ ] Dirty working tree is detected and rejected before any checkouts happen.
- [ ] Final report includes commit hash, author, commit message, and regression magnitude.

title: Evaluate ClickHouse Cloud ($300 promo credits pending)
description:
## Objective

Evaluate ClickHouse Cloud as an alternative to Elestio for Rafn's ClickHouse hosting, contingent on receiving $300 promotional credits.

## Context

* ClickHouse Cloud Basic tier starts at \~$99/month (dev) with consumption-based billing
* Previously requested $300 promo credits — awaiting response
* If credits are granted, this gives \~3 months of free usage for prototyping
* If credits are denied, deprioritize this ticket and proceed with Elestio ([VRN-25](https://linear.app/voron-software/issue/VRN-25/procure-elestio-managed-clickhouse-instance))

## Evaluation criteria

1. **Idle behavior** — does the service auto-pause and stop billing when no queries are running?
2. **Resume latency** — how long does cold-start take after idle? (docs say 20-30s)
3. **SharedMergeTree portability** — CH Cloud uses SharedMergeTree by default; verify data can be exported to standard ReplicatedMergeTree for self-hosted fallback
4. **Compute cost under Rafn's workload** — estimate monthly cost for realistic ingestion patterns (small batch inserts from CI runs, periodic dashboard queries)
5. **API/driver compatibility** — confirm the clickhouse-rs crate works without issues against CH Cloud

## Decision gate

If credits arrive → spin up a Basic tier service and run evaluation in parallel with Elestio.
If no credits → close this ticket, proceed with Elestio only.

title: Benchmark Corpus: Fork and maintain reference OSS projects per language
description:
## Goal

Maintain a corpus of forked open-source projects — one per supported language — that have extensive benchmark suites. These forks run benchmarks in GitHub Actions on a schedule, producing real-world data that Rafn ingests for dogfooding, demos, and integration testing.

## Selected projects

| Language | Project | Benchmark tool | Repo |
| -- | -- | -- | -- |
| Rust | regex (BurntSushi) | Criterion | [github.com/BurntSushi/regex](<http://github.com/BurntSushi/regex>) |
| Go | Prometheus | testing.B | [github.com/prometheus/prometheus](<http://github.com/prometheus/prometheus>) |
| Java | Jackson benchmarks | JMH | [github.com/FasterXML/jackson-benchmarks](<http://github.com/FasterXML/jackson-benchmarks>) |
| C# | MessagePack-CSharp | BenchmarkDotNet | [github.com/MessagePack-CSharp/MessagePack-CSharp](<http://github.com/MessagePack-CSharp/MessagePack-CSharp>) |

## Selection criteria

* Standard benchmark tooling for each language
* 20+ benchmark methods minimum
* Actively maintained upstream
* Reasonable GH Actions runtime (<30 min)

## Per-language work

1. Fork upstream repo
2. Add GH Actions workflow running benchmarks on schedule (nightly)
3. Add Rafn CLI upload step
4. Verify output format is parseable by Rafn ingestion
5. Document upstream-specific quirks
